questions.question_text: If X is 90% of Y, then what percent of X is Y?
questions.solution:
X = $ rac{90}{100}Y 	imes X =  rac{9}{10}Y 	imes Y =  rac{10}{9}X 	imes Y =  rac{10}{9}X$.
Required percentage = $( rac{Y}{X}	imes100)$ % = $( rac{10}{9}	imes100)$ % = $111 rac{1}{9}$%.

questions.question_text: x% of y is y% of
questions.solution: x% of y = $ rac{x}{100}	imes y =  rac{y}{100}	imes x = y$% of x.

questions.question_text: If 20% of a = b, then b% of 20 is the same as
questions.solution:
$20\%$ of $a = b \Rightarrow \frac{20}{100}a = b$.

$\therefore b\%$ of $20 = \frac{b}{100} \times 20 = \left(\frac{20}{100} \times a \times \frac{1}{20}\right) \times 20$

$= \frac{4}{100}a = 4\%$ of $a$.

questions.question_text: If x% of y is the same as $ rac{4}{5}$ of 80, then the value of xy is
questions.solution: $\frac{x}{100} \times y = \frac{4}{5} \times 80 \Rightarrow xy = 64 \times 100 = 6400$.

questions.question_text: If x% of y is 100 and y% of z is 200, find a relation between x and z.
questions.solution: Clearly, $y\%$ of $z = 2(x\%$ of $y) \Rightarrow \frac{yz}{100} = \frac{2xy}{100} \Rightarrow z = 2x$.

questions.question_text: If x% of 500 = y% of 300 and x% of y% of 200 = 60, then x =?
questions.solution:
$x\%$ of $500 = y\%$ of $300$

$\Rightarrow \frac{x}{100} \times 500 = \frac{y}{100} \times 300 \Rightarrow 5x = 3y \Rightarrow y = \frac{5}{3}x$.

$x\%$ of $y\%$ of $200 = 60 \Rightarrow \frac{x}{100} \times \frac{y}{100} \times 200 = 60$

$\Rightarrow xy = 3000 \Rightarrow x \times \frac{5}{3}x = 3000$

$\Rightarrow x^2 = 3000 \times \frac{3}{5} = 1800 \Rightarrow x = 30\sqrt{2}$.

questions.question_text: If x, y, z are three positive integers such that x is greater than y and y is greater than z, then which of the following is definitely true?
questions.option_a: x% of y is greater than y% of z
questions.option_b: y% of x is greater than z% of y
questions.option_c: z% of x is greater than y% of z
questions.solution:
(a) $x\%$ of $y = \frac{xy}{100}$ and $y\%$ of $z = \frac{yz}{100}$.

$x > y, y > z \Rightarrow xy > yz \Rightarrow x\%$ of $y > y\%$ of $z$.

(b) $y\%$ of $x = \frac{xy}{100}$ and $z\%$ of $y = \frac{yz}{100}$.

As proved above, $y\%$ of $x > z\%$ of $y$.

(c) $z\%$ of $x = \frac{xz}{100}$ and $y\%$ of $z = \frac{yz}{100}$.

$x > y \Rightarrow xz > yz \Rightarrow z\%$ of $x > y\%$ of $z$.

questions.question_text: If 20% of A = 50% of B, what percentage of A is B?
questions.solution:
$20\%$ of $A = 50\%$ of $B \Rightarrow \frac{20}{100} \times A = \frac{50}{100} \times B$

$\Rightarrow \frac{A}{5} = \frac{B}{2} \Rightarrow B = \frac{2A}{5}$.

Required percentage = $\left(\frac{B}{A} \times 100\right) \%$

$= \left(\frac{2A}{5} \times \frac{1}{A} \times 100\right) \% = 40\%$.

questions.question_text: If p% of p is 36, then p is equal to
questions.solution: $p\%$ of $p = 36 \Leftrightarrow \frac{p}{100} \times p = 36 \Leftrightarrow p^2 = 3600 \Leftrightarrow p = 60$.

questions.question_text: If x% of y is equal to z, what percent of z is x?
questions.solution:
$x\%$ of $y = z \Rightarrow \frac{x}{100} \times y = z \Rightarrow \frac{x}{z} = \frac{100}{y}$.

$\therefore$ Required percentage = $\left(\frac{x}{z} \times 100\right) \%$

$= \left(\frac{100}{y} \times 100\right) \% = \left(\frac{100^2}{y}\right) \%$.

questions.question_text: If x is 80% of y, then what percent of 2x is y?
questions.solution:
$x = 80\%$ of $y \Rightarrow x = \frac{80}{100}y \Leftrightarrow \frac{y}{x} = \frac{5}{4} \Rightarrow \frac{y}{2x} = \frac{5}{8}$.

$\therefore$ Required percentage = $\left(\frac{y}{2x} \times 100\right) \%$

$= \left(\frac{5}{8} \times 100\right) \% = 62\frac{1}{2}\%$.

questions.question_text: Subtracting 6% of x from x is equivalent to multiplying x by how much?
questions.solution:
Let $x - 6\%$ of $x = xz$.

Then, $94\%$ of $x = xz \Leftrightarrow \frac{94}{100} \times x = xz \Leftrightarrow z = 0.94$.

questions.question_text: (x% of y + y% of x) =?
x% of x is the same as 10% of
questions.solution:
Let $x\%$ of $x = 10\%$ of $y$.

Then, $\frac{x}{100} \times x = \frac{10}{100} \times y \Rightarrow y = \frac{x^2}{10}$.

questions.question_text: If a exceeds b by x%, then which one of the following equations is correct?
questions.solution: $A$ exceeds $b$ by $x\% \Rightarrow a = b + x\%$ of $b \Rightarrow a = b + \frac{bx}{100}$.

questions.question_text: If A is 150 percent of B, then B is what percent of (A + B)?
questions.solution:
$A = 150\%$ of $B \Rightarrow A = \frac{150}{100}B \Rightarrow \frac{A}{B} = \frac{3}{2} \Rightarrow \frac{A}{B} + 1 = \frac{3}{2} + 1$

$\Rightarrow \frac{A+B}{B} = \frac{5}{2} \Rightarrow \frac{B}{A+B} = \frac{2}{5}$.

$\therefore$ Required percentage = $\left(\frac{B}{A+B} \times 100\right) \%$

$= \left(\frac{2}{5} \times 100\right) \% = 40\%$.

questions.question_text: If 8% of x = 4% of y, then 20% of x is
questions.solution:
$8\%$ of $x = 4\%$ of $y \Rightarrow \frac{8}{100}x = \frac{4}{100}y \Rightarrow y = 2x$.

$\therefore 20\%$ of $x = 20\%$ of $\frac{y}{2} = 10\%$ of $y$.

questions.question_text: If 20% of A = B and 40% of B = C, then 60% of (A + B) is
questions.solution:
$\frac{20}{100}A = B$ and $\frac{40}{100}B = C \Rightarrow \frac{A}{5} = B$ and $\frac{2B}{5} = C$

$\Rightarrow A = 5B$ and $B = \frac{5}{2}C \Rightarrow A = 5 \times \frac{5}{2}C = \frac{25}{2}C$ and $B = \frac{5}{2}C$.

$\therefore 60\%$ of $(A + B) = \frac{60}{100} \left(\frac{25}{2}C + \frac{5}{2}C\right)$

$= \frac{60}{100} \times \frac{30}{2}C = \frac{60 \times 15}{100}C = 900\%$ of $C$.

questions.question_text: If x% of a is the same as y% of b, then z% of b is
questions.solution:
$x\%$ of $a = y\%$ of $b \Rightarrow \frac{x}{100}a = \frac{y}{100}b \Rightarrow b = \frac{x}{y}a$.

$\therefore z\%$ of $b = z\%$ of $\left(\frac{x}{y}a\right) = \frac{z}{100} \times \frac{x}{y}a = \frac{xz}{y} \%$ of $a$.

questions.question_text: If A = x% of y and B = y% of x, then which of the following is true?
questions.option_c: Relationship between A and B cannot be determined.
questions.option_d: If x is smaller than y, then A is greater than B.
questions.solution: $x\%$ of $y = y\%$ of $x \Rightarrow \frac{xy}{100} = \frac{yx}{100} \Rightarrow A = B$.

questions.question_text: If 50% of (x – y) = 30% of (x + y), then what percent of x is y?
questions.solution:
$50\%$ of $(x - y) = 30\%$ of $(x + y)$

$\Rightarrow 5(x - y) = 3(x + y)$

$\Rightarrow 5x - 5y = 3x + 3y \Rightarrow 2x = 8y \Rightarrow y = \frac{x}{4}$.

$\therefore$ Required percentage = $\left(\frac{y}{x} \times 100\right) \%$

$= \left(\frac{x}{4} \times \frac{1}{x} \times 100\right) \% = 25\%$.

questions.question_text: If a is 60% of b, then what percent of 4a is 5b?
questions.solution:
$a = 60\%$ of $b \Rightarrow a = \frac{60}{100}b \Rightarrow b = \frac{5}{3}a$.

$\therefore$ Required percentage = $\left(\frac{5}{4}b \times 100\right) \%$

$= \left(\frac{5}{4} \times \frac{5}{3}a \times 100\right) \% = \frac{625}{3}\%$.

questions.question_text: If x = 63% of y, then $y^2$ is approximately what percent of $x^2$?
questions.solution:
x = 63% of y $\Rightarrow$ x = $\frac{63}{100}$ y $\Rightarrow$ y = $\frac{100}{63}$ x.

Required percentage = $\left(\frac{y^2}{x^2}\times100\right)$%

questions.question_text: If $a = b 	imes  rac{d}{c}$, and $b, c$ and $d$ are each increased by 10%, then by how much does a increase?
questions.solution:
Let B = 110% of b = $\frac{11}{10}$ b; C = 110% of c = $\frac{11}{10}$ c;
D = 110% of d = $\frac{11}{10}$ d
Then, A = B $\times$ $\frac{D}{C}$ = $\frac{11}{10}$ b $\times$ $\frac{11}{10}$ d $\times$ $\frac{10}{11c}$ = $\frac{11bd}{10c}$
= $\frac{11}{10}$ a = 110% of a.
Increase % = 10%.

questions.question_text: If a% of x is equal to b% of y, then c% of y is what % of x?
questions.solution:
a% of x = b% of y $\Rightarrow$ $\frac{ax}{100}$ = $\frac{by}{100}$ $\Rightarrow$ y = $\frac{ax}{b}$.
Required percentage = $\left[\frac{c\%\text{ of }y}{x}\times100\right]$%
= $\left(\frac{c}{100}\times\frac{ax}{b}\times\frac{1}{x}\times100\right)$% = $\frac{ac}{b}$%.

questions.question_text: The firm uses the following function to calculate the production output (PO): $PO = 5.3 C^2 L^{15}$, where C = capital invested and L = labour employed. If the capital invested (C) increases by 20 percent, the change in PO will be

A company received two shipments of ball bearings. In the first shipment, 1 percent of the ball bearings were defective. In the second shipment, which was twice as large as the first, 4.5 percent of the ball bearings were defective. If the company received a total of 100 defective ball bearings, how many ball bearings were in the first shipment?
questions.solution:
Let the total number of ball bearings in the first shipment be x.
Then, total number of ball bearings in the second shipment = 2x.
1% of x + 4.5% of 2x = 100 $\Rightarrow$ $\frac{x}{100}$ + $\frac{9x}{100}$ = 100
10x = 10000
x = 1000.

questions.question_text: In a graduate class of 200, 40% are women and $ rac{1}{5}$ become lecturers. If the number of men who become lecturers is twice that of women, calculate approximate percentage of men who became lecturers.
questions.solution:
Number of women = 40% of 200 = 80.
Number of men = 200 - 80 = 120.
Number of women lecturers = $\frac{1}{5}$ of 80 = 16.
Number of men lecturers = 16 $\times$ 2 = 32.
Required percentage = $\left(\frac{32}{120}\times100\right)$%
= 26 $\frac{2}{3}$% $\approx$ 27%.

questions.question_text: The contents of a certain box consist of 14 apples and 23 oranges. How many oranges must be removed from the box so that 70% of the pieces of fruit in the box will be apples?
questions.solution:
Total number of fruits = (14 + 23) = 37.
Let x oranges be removed.
Then, 70% of (37 - x) = 14
7(37 - x) = 140
37 - x = 20 $\Rightarrow$ x = 17.

questions.question_text: The weight of an empty bucket is 25% of the weight of the bucket when filled with some liquid. Some of the liquid has been removed. Then, the bucket, along with the remaining liquid, weighed three-fifths of the original weight. What percentage of the liquid has been removed?
questions.solution:
Let the weight of the bucket when it is full, be 1 kg.
Then, weight of empty bucket = 25% of 1 kg = $\frac{1}{4}$ kg.
Weight of liquid in the bucket = $\left(1 - \frac{1}{4}\right)$ kg = $\frac{3}{4}$ kg.
On removing the liquid,
Weight of (bucket + liquid) = $\frac{3}{5}$ kg.
Weight of liquid in the bucket = $\left(\frac{3}{5} - \frac{1}{4}\right)$ kg = $\frac{7}{20}$ kg.
Weigh of liquid removed = $\left(\frac{3}{4} - \frac{7}{20}\right)$ kg = $\frac{8}{20}$ kg = $\frac{2}{5}$ kg.
Hence, required percentage = $\left(\frac{2/5}{3/4}\times100\right)$%
= $\left(\frac{2}{5}\times\frac{4}{3}\times100\right)$% = 53 $\frac{1}{3}$%.

questions.question_text: A part of ₹ 9600 is invested at a 5% annual return, while the remainder is invested at a 3% annual return. If the annual income from both portions is the same, what is the total income from the two investments?
questions.solution:
Let the sum invested at 5% be ₹ x and that invested at 3% be ₹ (9600 - x).
Then, 5% of x = 3% of (9600 - x)
5x = 3(9600 - x)
8x = 28800 $\Rightarrow$ x = 3600.
Hence, total income = 5% of x + 3% of (9600 - x)
= ₹ (5% of 3600 + 3% of 6000)
= ₹ (180 + 180) = ₹ 360.

questions.question_text: A salesman’s commission is 5% on all sales up to ₹ 10000 and 4% of all sales exceeding this amount. He remits ₹ 31100 to the parent company after deducting his commission. His sales were worth

In a co-educational school there are 15 more girls than boys. If the number of girls is increased by 10% and the number of boys is also increased by 16%, there would be nine more girls than boys. What is the number of students in the school?
questions.solution:
Let the number of boys be x. Then, number of girls = x + 15.
110% of (x + 15) - 116% of x = 9
1.1(x + 15) - 1.16x = 9
1.1x + 16.5 - 1.16x = 9
0.06x = 7.5 $\Rightarrow$ x = 125.
Hence, number of students in the school = x + (x + 15) = 2x + 15 = 2(125) + 15 = 265.

questions.question_text: 5 kg of tea and 8 kg of sugar together cost ₹ 172. The price of tea has risen by 20% and that of sugar by 10%. Hence the same quantities of tea and sugar now cost ₹ 199.20. What is the original price of tea per kg?
questions.solution:
Let the original price of tea be ₹ x per kg and that of sugar be ₹ y per kg.
5x + 8y = 172 $\Rightarrow$ 15x + 24y = 516 ... (i)
120% of 5x + 110% of 8y = 199.20
6x + 8.8y = 199.20 $\Rightarrow$ 15x + 22y = 498 ... (ii)
Subtracting (ii) from (i), we get: 2y = 18 or y = 9.
Putting y = 9 in (i), we get: x = 20.

questions.question_text: 605 sweets were distributed equally among children in such a way that the number of sweets received by each child is 20% of the total number of children. How many sweets did each child receive?
questions.solution:
Let the total number of children be x.
Then, x $\times$ (20% of x) = 605
$\frac{x^2}{5}$ = 605 $\Rightarrow$ $x^2$ = 3025 $\Rightarrow$ x = 55.

questions.question_text: In a certain organisation, 40% employees are matriculates, 50% of the remaining are graduates and the remaining 180 are post-graduates. What is the number of graduate employees?
questions.solution:
Let the total number of employees be x.
(100 - 50)% of (100 - 40)% of x = 180 $\Rightarrow$ 50% of 60% of x = 180
$\frac{50}{100}\times\frac{60}{100}\times x$ = 180 $\Rightarrow$ 0.3x = 180 $\Rightarrow$ x = 600.
Number of graduate employees = 50% of 60% of 600 = 180.

questions.question_text: Gaurav spends 30% of his monthly income on food articles, 40% of the remaining on conveyance and clothes and saves 50% of the remaining. If his monthly salary is ₹ 18,400, how much money does he save every month?

A spider climbed $62\frac{1}{2}\%$ of the height of the pole in one hour and in the next hour it covered $12\frac{1}{2}\%$ of the remaining height. If the height of the pole is 192 m, then distance climbed in second hour is
questions.solution:
Height climbed in second hour
= 12 $\frac{1}{2}$% of (100 - 62 $\frac{1}{2}$)% of 192 m
= $\left(\frac{25}{200}\times\frac{75}{100}\times192\right)$ m = 9 m.

questions.question_text: A man spends 35% of his income on food, 25% on children’s education and 80% of the remaining on house rent. What percent of his income he is left with?
questions.solution:
Let the total income be x.
Income left = (100 - 80)% of [100 - (35 + 25)]% of x = 20% of 40% of x.
= $\left(\frac{20}{100}\times\frac{40}{100}\right)$ of x = 8% of x.

questions.question_text: Mr. More spent 20% of his monthly income on food and 15% on children’s education. 40% of the remaining he spent on entertainment and transport together and 30% on medical. He is left with an amount of ₹ 8775 after all these expenditures. What is Mr. More’s monthly income?
questions.solution:
Let Mr. More's monthly income be ₹ x.
[100 - (40 + 30)]% of [100 - (20 + 15)]% of x = 8775
30% of 65% of x = 8775
$\left(\frac{30}{100}\times\frac{65}{100}\times x\right)$ = 8775
$\frac{39}{200}x$ = 8775 $\Rightarrow$ x = 45000.

questions.question_text: From the salary of an officer, 10% is deducted as house rent, 20% of the rest, he spends on conveyance, 20% of the rest he pays as income tax and 10% of the balance, he spends on clothes. Then, he is left with ₹ 15,552. Find his total salary.
questions.solution:
Let the total salary be ₹ x.
(100 - 10)% of (100 - 20)% of (100 - 20)% of (100 - 10)% of x = 15552
90% of 80% of 80% of 90% of x = 15552
$\left(\frac{90}{100}\times\frac{80}{100}\times\frac{80}{100}\times\frac{90}{100}\times x\right)$ = 15552
0.5184x = 15552 $\Rightarrow$ x = 30000.

questions.question_text: Aman gave 40% of the amount he had to Rohan. Rohan in turn gave one-fourth of what he received from Aman to Sahil. After paying ₹ 200 to the taxi driver out of the amount he got from Rohan, Sahil now has ₹ 600 left with him. How much amount did Aman have?
questions.solution:
Let the amount with Aman be ₹ x.
Amount received by Sahil = $\frac{1}{4}$ of 40% of ₹ x = 10% of ₹ x.
10% of x = 600 + 200
10% of x = 800 $\Rightarrow$ x = 8000.

questions.question_text: On a test consisting of 250 questions, Jassi answered 40% of the first 125 questions correctly. What percent of the other 125 questions does she need to answer correctly for her grade on the entire exam to be 60%?
questions.solution:
Number of questions answered correctly = 40% of 125 = 50.
For 60% grade, number of questions to be answered correctly = 60% of 250 = 150.
Remaining number of questions to be answered correctly = 150 - 50 = 100.

Required percentage = $\left(\frac{100}{125}\times100\right)\% = 80\%$.

questions.question_text: In a certain month a baseball team that played 60 games had won 30% of its games played. After a phenomenal winning streak this team raised its average to 50%. How many games must the team have won in a row to attain this average?
questions.solution:
Number of games already won = 30% of 60 = 18.
Let the required number of games be x.
Then, $\frac{18+x}{60+x}\times100 = 50 \Rightarrow \frac{18+x}{60+x} = \frac{1}{2}$
$\Rightarrow 36 + 2x = 60 + x \Rightarrow x = 24$.

questions.question_text: The sum of the number of boys and girls in a school is 150. If the number of boys is x, then the number of girls becomes x% of the total number of students. The number of boys is
questions.solution:
We have: $x + x\% \text{ of } 150 = 150$
$\Leftrightarrow x + \frac{x}{100}\times150 = 150 \Leftrightarrow x + \frac{5}{2}x = 150 \Leftrightarrow \left(\frac{150\times2}{5}\right) = 60$.

questions.question_text: In an examination of n questions, a student replied 15 out of the first 20 questions correctly. Of the remaining questions, he answered one-third correctly. All the questions have the same credit. If the student gets 50% marks, the value of n is
questions.solution:
$15 + \frac{1}{3}(n-20) = 50\% \text{ of } n = \frac{50}{100}n = \frac{n}{2}$
$\Leftrightarrow 90 + 2n - 40 = 3n \Leftrightarrow n = 50$.

questions.question_text: The salaries of A and B together amount to ₹ 2000. A spends 95% of his salary and B, 85% of his. If now, their savings are the same, what is A’s salary?
questions.solution:
Let A's salary = ₹ x. Then, B's salary = ₹ (2000 - x).
(100 - 95)% of A = (100 - 85)% of B
$\Rightarrow \frac{5}{100}x = \frac{15}{100}(2000 - x) \Leftrightarrow x = 1500$.

questions.question_text: A’s marks in Biology are 20 less than 25% of the total marks obtained by him in Biology, Maths and Drawing. If his marks in Drawing be 50, what are his marks in Maths?
questions.solution:
Let B + M + D = x. Then, B = 25% of x - 20
$= \left(\frac{25}{100}x - 20\right) = \left(\frac{x}{4} - 20\right)$ and D = 50.
$\therefore \left(\frac{x}{4} - 20 + M + 50\right) = x \text{ or } M = \left(\frac{3x}{4} - 30\right)$.
So, marks in Maths cannot be determined.

questions.question_text: In an examination, there are three papers and a candidate has to get 35% of the total to pass. In one paper, he gets 62 out of 150 and in the second 35 out of 150. How much must he get, out of 180, in the third paper to just qualify for a pass?
questions.solution:
Let the marks required be x. Then, (62 + 35 + x) = 35% of (150 + 150 + 180)
$\Leftrightarrow 97 + x = \frac{35}{100}\times480 \Leftrightarrow x = 168 - 97 = 71$.

questions.question_text: In a History examination, the average for the entire class was 80 marks. If 10% of the students scored 95 marks and 20% scored 90 marks, what was the average marks of the remaining students of the class?
questions.solution:
Let the number of students in the class be 100 and let the required average be x.
Then, (10 $\times$ 95) + (20 $\times$ 90) + (70 $\times$ x) = (100 $\times$ 80)
$\Rightarrow 70x = 8000 - (950 + 1800) = 5250 \Leftrightarrow x = 75$.

questions.question_text: A scored 30% marks and failed by 15 marks. B scored 40% marks and obtained 35 marks more than those required to pass. The pass percentage is
questions.solution:
Let total marks = x. Then, (30% of x) + 15 = (40% of x) - 35
$\Leftrightarrow \frac{30}{100}x + 15 = \frac{40}{100}x - 35 \Leftrightarrow \frac{10}{100}x = 50 \Leftrightarrow x = 500$.
So, passing marks = (30% of 500) + 15
$= \left(\frac{30}{100}\times500\right) + 15 = 165$.
$\therefore$ Pass percentage = $\left(\frac{165}{500}\times100\right)\% = 33\%$.

questions.question_text: In an area, of the total people 40% were women and 45% coffee drinkers. One-third of the males are coffee drinkers. Suppose the total number of persons in the area is 100, then the number of female non coffee drinkers is
questions.solution:
Number of women = 40% of 100 = 40.
Number of men = (100 - 40) = 60.
Number of coffee-drinkers = 45% of 100 = 45.
Number of male coffee-drinkers = $\frac{1}{3}$ of 60 = 20.
Number of female coffee-drinkers = (45 - 20) = 25.
$\therefore$ Number of female non-coffee drinkers = (40 - 25) = 15.

questions.question_text: A city has a population of 3,00,000 out of which 1,80,000 are males. 50% of the population is literate. If 70% of the males are literate, then the percentage of females who are literate is
questions.solution:
Number of males = 180000. Number of females = (300000 - 180000) = 120000.
Number of literates = 50% of 300000 = 150000.
Number of literate males = 70% of 180000 = 126000.
Number of literate females = (150000 - 126000) = 24000.
$\therefore$ Required percentage = $\left(\frac{24000}{120000}\times100\right)\% = 20\%$.

questions.question_text: In a company there are 75% skilled workers and the remaining are unskilled. 80% of skilled workers and 20% of unskilled workers are permanent. If the number of temporary workers is 126, then what is the total number of workers?
questions.solution:
Let the total number of workers be x. Then, number of skilled workers = 75% of x = $\frac{3x}{4}$.
Number of unskilled workers = $\left(x - \frac{3x}{4}\right) = \frac{x}{4}$.
Number of temporary workers = (100 - 80)% of $\frac{3x}{4} + (100 - 20)% of \frac{x}{4}$
= 20% of $\frac{3x}{4} + 80\%$ of $\frac{x}{4} = \left(\frac{20}{100}\times\frac{3x}{4} + \frac{80}{100}\times\frac{x}{4}\right) = \frac{7x}{20}$.
$\frac{7x}{20} = 126 \Rightarrow x = \left(\frac{126\times20}{7}\right) = 360$.

questions.question_text: A clothing supplier stores 800 coats in a warehouse, of which 15 percent are full-length coats. If 500 of the short-length coats are removed form the warehouse, then what percent of the remaining coats are full length?
questions.solution:
Number of short-length coats before removal = (100 - 15)% of 800 = 85% of 800 = $\left(\frac{85}{100}\times800\right) = 680$.
Number of short-length coats after removal = (680 - 500) = 180.
Total number of coats after removal = (800 - 500) = 300.
Number of full-length coats after removal = (300 - 180) = 120.
$\therefore$ Required percentage = $\left(\frac{120}{300}\times100\right)\% = 40\%$.

questions.question_text: At the college entrance examination, each candidate is admitted or rejected according to whether he has passed or failed the tests. Of the candidates who are really capable, 80% pass the tests and of the incapable, 25% pass the test. Given that 40% of the candidates are really capable, the proportion of capable college students is about
questions.solution:
Let the total number of candidates be x.
Number of capable candidates who got admitted to college = 80% of 40% of x = $\left(\frac{80}{100}\times\frac{40}{100}\times x\right) = \frac{8x}{25}$.
Number of incapable college students = 25% of (100 - 40)% of x = $\left(\frac{25}{100}\times\frac{60}{100}\times x\right) = \frac{3x}{20}$.
Total number of candidates who got admitted to college = $\frac{8x}{25} + \frac{3x}{20} = \frac{47x}{100}$.
$\therefore$ Required percentage = $\left(\frac{8x}{25}\times\frac{100}{47x}\times100\right)\% = \frac{3200}{47}\% = 68.09\% \approx 68\%$.

questions.question_text: In a public school, $\frac{1}{5}$ of girls and $\frac{1}{4}$ of boys are under 12 years of age. If the number of girls is $\frac{2}{5}$ of the total, what part of the total strength of the school is accounted for by those who are 12 years or more of age?
questions.solution:
Let the total strength of the school be x.
Number of girls = $\frac{2x}{5}$; Number of boys = $\left(x - \frac{2x}{5}\right) = \frac{3x}{5}$.
Number of students who are 12 years or more of age = $\left(1 - \frac{1}{5}\right)$ of $\frac{2x}{5} + \left(1 - \frac{1}{4}\right)$ of $\frac{3x}{5}$
= $\left(\frac{4}{5}\times\frac{2x}{5} + \frac{3}{4}\times\frac{3x}{5}\right) = \left(\frac{8x}{25} + \frac{9x}{20}\right) = \frac{77x}{100}$.
$\therefore$ Required percentage = $\left(\frac{77x}{100}\times\frac{1}{x}\times100\right)\% = 77\%$.

questions.question_text: In a city, 35% of the population is composed of migrants, 20% of whom are from rural areas. Of the local population, 48% is female while this figure for rural and urban migrants is 30% and 40% respectively. What percent of the total population comprises of females?
questions.solution:
Let the total population be x.
Then, migrant population = 35% of x = $\frac{7x}{20}$.
Local population = $\left(x - \frac{7x}{20}\right) = \frac{13x}{20}$.
Number of rural migrants = 20% of $\frac{7x}{20} = \left(\frac{20}{100}\times\frac{7x}{20}\right) = \frac{7x}{100}$.
Number of urban migrants = $\left(\frac{7x}{20} - \frac{7x}{100}\right) = \frac{28x}{100} = \frac{7x}{25}$.
Female population = 48% of $\left(\frac{13x}{20} + 30\% \text{ of } \frac{7x}{100} + 40\% \text{ of } \frac{7x}{25}\right)$
= $\left(\frac{48}{100}\times\frac{13x}{20} + \frac{30}{100}\times\frac{7x}{100} + \frac{40}{100}\times\frac{7x}{25}\right) = \left(\frac{39x}{125} + \frac{21x}{1000} + \frac{14x}{125}\right) = \frac{445x}{1000}$.
$\therefore$ Required percentage = $\left(\frac{445x}{1000}\times\frac{1}{x}\times100\right)\% = 44.5\%$.

questions.question_text: In a recent survey, 40% houses contained two or more people. Of those houses containing only one person, 25% were having only a male. What is the percentage of all houses, which contain exactly one female and no males?
questions.solution:
Let the total number of houses be x. Then,
Number of houses having one female only = (100 - 25)% of (100 - 40)% of x = $\left(\frac{75}{100}\times\frac{60}{100}\right)x = \frac{9x}{20}$.
$\therefore$ Required percentage = $\left(\frac{9x}{20}\times\frac{1}{x}\times100\right)\% = 45\%$.

questions.question_text: $37\frac{1}{2}\%$ of the candidates in an examination were girls, 75% of the boys and $62\frac{1}{2}\%$ of the girls passed and 342 girls failed. The number of boys failed was:
questions.solution:
Let the total number of candidates be x.
Then, $\left(100 - 62\frac{1}{2}\right)\% \text{ of } 37\frac{1}{2}\% \text{ of } x = 342$
$\Leftrightarrow \frac{75}{2}\times\frac{1}{100}\times\frac{75}{2}\times\frac{1}{100}\times x = 342$
$\Leftrightarrow \frac{9}{64}x = 342 \Leftrightarrow x = \left(\frac{342\times64}{9}\right) = 2432$.
Number of boys failed = (100 - 75)% of $\left(100 - 37\frac{1}{2}\right)\% \text{ of } 2432 = \left(\frac{25}{100}\times\frac{125}{2}\times\frac{1}{100}\times2432\right) = 380$.

questions.question_text: $\frac{5}{9}$ part of the population in a village are males. If 30% of the males are married, the percentage of unmarried females in the total population is
questions.solution:
Let total population = x. Then, number of males = $\frac{5x}{9}$.
Married males = 30% of $\frac{5x}{9} = \left(\frac{30}{100}\times\frac{5x}{9}\right) = \frac{x}{6}$.
Married females = $\frac{x}{6}$;
Number of females = $\left(x - \frac{5x}{9}\right) = \frac{4x}{9}$.
Unmarried females = $\left(\frac{4x}{9} - \frac{x}{6}\right) = \frac{5x}{18}$.
$\therefore$ Required percentage = $\left(\frac{5x}{18}\times\frac{1}{x}\times100\right)\% = 27\frac{7}{9}\%$.

questions.question_text: The boys and girls in a college are in the ratio 3: 2. If 20% of the boys and 25% of the girls are adults, the percentage of students who are not adults is
questions.solution:
Let the number of boys and girls be 3x and 2x respectively.
Then,
No. of students who are not adults = $\left(\frac{80}{100}\times3x + \frac{75}{100}\times2x\right)$
= $\left(\frac{12x}{5} + \frac{3x}{2}\right) = \frac{39x}{10}$.
$\therefore$ Required percentage = $\left(\frac{39x}{10}\times\frac{1}{5x}\times100\right)\% = 78\%$.

questions.question_text: A debtor can pay 87 paise in the rupee, but if his creditors would take 20% of his debts, he could pay them and have ₹ 42 left. His debts and assets respectively are:
questions.solution:
Let total debt = $x$. Asset = $ rac{87}{100}x$.
After paying 20% of the debt, he is left with 80% of the debt plus ₹ 42.
$\therefore$ 80% of $x + 42 = \frac{87}{100}x \Leftrightarrow \frac{87}{100}x - \frac{80}{100}x = 42$
$x = 42 \Leftrightarrow x = 600$.
So, debt = ₹ 600 and assets = ₹ $\left(\frac{87}{100}\times600\right) = ₹ 522$.

questions.question_text: Of the 50 researchers in a workgroup, 40% will be assigned to Team A and the remaining 60% to Team B. However, 70% of the researchers prefer Team A and 30% prefer Team B. What is the least possible number of researchers who will not be assigned to the team they prefer?
questions.solution:
Number of researchers who prefer Team A = 70% of 50 = 35.
Number of researchers who prefer Team B = (50 - 35) = 15.
Number of researchers assigned to Team A = 40% of 50 = 20.
Number of researchers assigned to Team B = (50 - 20) = 30.
To find the least possible number of researchers who will not be assigned to the team they prefer, we assume that the maximum number of researchers get the team they prefer.
So, number of researchers who are assigned to the team they prefer
= 20 (Team A) + 15 (Team B) = 35.
$\therefore$ Required number = (50 - 35) = 15.

questions.question_text: A train starts from station A with some passengers. At station B 10% of the passengers get down and 100 passengers get in. At station C 50% get down and 25 get in. At station D 50% get down and 50 get in making the total number of passengers 200. The number of passengers who boarded the train at station A was
questions.solution:
Let the number of passengers who boarded the train at station A be $x$. Then,
Number of passengers after the train left station B
= (100 - 10)% of $x + 100 = 90\%$ of $x + 100 = \left(\frac{9}{10}x + 100\right)$.
Number of passengers after the train left station C
= (100 - 50)% of $\left(\frac{9}{10}x + 100\right) + 25 = \frac{50}{100}\left(\frac{9}{10}x + 100\right) + 25$
= $\left(\frac{9}{20}x + 75\right)$.
Number of passengers after the train left station D
= (100 - 50)% of $\left(\frac{9}{20}x + 75\right) + 50$
= $\frac{50}{100}\left(\frac{9}{20}x + 75\right) + 50 = \left(\frac{9}{40}x + \frac{175}{2}\right)$.
$\therefore \frac{9}{40}x + \frac{175}{2} = 200 \Rightarrow \frac{9}{40}x = 200 - \frac{175}{2} = \frac{225}{2}$.
$\Rightarrow x = \left(\frac{225}{2} \times \frac{40}{9}\right) = 500$.

questions.question_text: The charges for a five-day trip by a tourist bus for one full ticket and a half-ticket are ₹ 1440 inclusive of boarding charges which are same for a full ticket and a half-ticket. The charges for the same trip for 2 full tickets and one half-ticket inclusive of boarding charges are ₹ 2220. The fare for a half-ticket is 75% of the full ticket. Find the fare and the boarding charges separately for one full ticket.
questions.solution:
Let the fare for a full ticket be ₹ $x$ and the boarding charges be ₹ $y$ per ticket.
Then, fare for a half-ticket = 75% of ₹ $x$ = ₹ $\frac{3}{4}x$.
$\therefore (x + y) + \left(\frac{3}{4}x + y\right) = 1440 \Rightarrow \frac{7}{4}x + 2y = 1440$
$\Rightarrow 7x + 8y = 5760$ ...(i)
And, $2(x + y) + \left(\frac{3}{4}x + y\right) = 2220$
$\Rightarrow \frac{11}{4}x + 3y = 2220$
$\Rightarrow 11x + 12y = 8880$ ...(ii)
Solving (i) and (ii), we have: $x = 480, y = 300$.

questions.question_text: Asha’s monthly income is 60% of Deepak’s monthly income and 120% of Maya’s monthly income. What is Maya’s monthly income if Deepak’s monthly income is ₹ 78000?
questions.solution:
Asha’s monthly income = 60% of ₹ 78000
= ₹ $\left(\frac{60}{100} \times 78000\right) = ₹ 46800$.
Let Maya’s monthly income be ₹ $x$.
Then, 120% of $x = 46800 \Rightarrow x = \left(\frac{46800 \times 100}{120}\right) = ₹ 39000$.

questions.question_text: Raju’s monthly salary is 20 percent more than Anuj’s monthly salary. Ravi’s monthly salary is ₹ 1500 more than Anuj’s salary. The sum of Raju’s, Ravi’s and Anuj’s yearly salaries is ₹ 325200. What is the sum of the monthly salaries of Raju and Anuj together?
questions.solution:
Let Anuj’s monthly salary be ₹ $x$. Then, Raju’s monthly salary = 120% of ₹ $x$ = ₹ $\frac{6}{5}x$.
Ravi’s monthly salary = ₹ $(x + 1500)$.
$\therefore \frac{6}{5}x + (x + 1500) + x = \frac{325200}{12} = 27100$
$\Rightarrow 16x + 7500 = 135500$
$\Rightarrow 16x = 128000 \Rightarrow x = 8000$.
Hence, sum of Raju’s and Anuj’s monthly salaries
= ₹ $\left(\frac{6}{5}x + x\right) = \frac{11}{5} \times 8000 = ₹ 17600$.

questions.question_text: A sum of ₹ 2236 is divided among A, B and C in such a way that A receives 25% more than C and C receives 25% less than B. What is A’s share in the amount?
questions.solution:
Let the amount received by B be ₹ $x$.
Then, amount received by C = 75% of ₹ $x$ = ₹ $\frac{3}{4}x$.
And, amount received by A = 125% of ₹ $\frac{3}{4}x$ = ₹ $\frac{15}{16}x$.
$\therefore \frac{15}{16}x + x + \frac{3}{4}x = 2236 \Rightarrow \frac{43}{16}x = 2236$
$\Rightarrow x = \left(\frac{2236 \times 16}{43}\right) = 832$.
Hence, A’s share = ₹ $\left(\frac{15}{16} \times 832\right) = ₹ 780$.

questions.question_text: A sum of ₹ 6100 was divided among 8 men, 10 women and 12 children in such a way that each man received 25% more than a woman and each woman received 25% more than a child. How much did each woman receive?
questions.solution:
Let the amount received by each child be ₹ $x$.
Then, amount received by each woman = 125% of ₹ $x$ = ₹ $\frac{5}{4}x$.
Amount received by each man = 125% of ₹ $\frac{5}{4}x$ = ₹ $\frac{25}{16}x$.
$\therefore 8 \times \frac{25}{16}x + 10 \times \frac{5}{4}x + 12x = 6100$
$\Rightarrow \frac{25}{2}x + \frac{25}{2}x + 12x = 6100 \Rightarrow 74x = 6100 \times 2$
$\Rightarrow x = \left(\frac{6100 \times 2}{74}\right)$.
Hence, amount received by each woman
= ₹ $\left(\frac{5}{4} \times \frac{6100 \times 2}{74}\right) = ₹ \frac{7625}{37} = ₹ 206.08$.

questions.question_text: In an examination in which full marks were 800, A gets 20% more than B, B gets 20% more than C, and C gets 15% less than D. If A got 576, what percentage of full marks did D get (approximately)?

In an examination, the percentage of students qualified to the number of students appeared from school A is 70%. In school B, the number of students appeared is 20% more than the students appeared from school A and the number of students qualified from school B is 50% more than the students qualified from school A. What is the percentage of students qualified to the number of students appeared from school B?
questions.solution:
Let number of students appeared from school A = 100.
Then, number of students qualified from school A = 70.
Number of students appeared from school B = 120.
Number of students qualified from school B
= $\left(\frac{150}{100} \times 70\right) = 105$.
$\therefore$ Required percentage = $\left(\frac{105}{120} \times 100\right)\% = 87.5\%$.

questions.question_text: If the price of a book is first decreased by 25% and then increased by 20%, then the net change in the price will be
questions.solution:
Let the original price be ₹ 100.
New final price = 120% of (75% of ₹ 100)
= ₹ $\left(\frac{120}{100} \times \frac{75}{100} \times 100\right) = ₹ 90$.
$\therefore$ Decrease = 10%.

questions.question_text: The price of a shirt is increased by 15% and then reduced by 15%. The final price of the shirt is
questions.solution:
Let the original price be ₹ 100.
New final price = 85% of (115% of ₹ 100)
= ₹ $\left(\frac{85}{100} \times \frac{115}{100} \times 100\right) = ₹ 97.75$.
$\therefore$ Decrease = (100 - 97.75)% = 2.25%.

questions.question_text: A number is first decreased by 10% and then increased by 10%. The number so obtained is 50 less than the original number. The original number is
questions.solution:
Let the original number be $x$.
Final number obtained = 110% of (90% of $x$)
= $\left(\frac{110}{100} \times \frac{90}{100} \times x\right) = \frac{99}{100}x$.
$\therefore x - \frac{99}{100}x = 50 \Leftrightarrow \frac{1}{100}x = 50 \Leftrightarrow x = 50 \times 100 = 5000$.

questions.question_text: A shopkeeper first increased the price of an article by 25% and then by 20%. What is the total percent increase?
questions.solution:
Let the original price be ₹ 100.
New final price = 120% of (125% of ₹ 100)
= ₹ $\left(\frac{120}{100} \times \frac{125}{100} \times 100\right) = ₹ 150$.
$\therefore$ Increase = (150 - 100)% = 50%.

questions.question_text: Two successive price increases of 10% each on an article are equivalent to a single price increase of
questions.solution:
Let the original price be ₹ 100.
New final price = 110% of (110% of ₹ 100)
= ₹ $\left(\frac{110}{100} \times \frac{110}{100} \times 100\right) = ₹ 121$.
$\therefore$ Total increase = (121 - 100)% = 21%.

questions.question_text: The price of an article was first increased by 10% and then again by 20%. If the last increased price be ₹ 33, the original price was
questions.solution:
Let the original price be ₹ $x$.
New final price = 120% of (110% of ₹ $x$)
= ₹ $\left(\frac{120}{100} \times \frac{110}{100} \times x\right) = ₹ \frac{132x}{100}$.
$\therefore \frac{132x}{100} = 33 \Rightarrow x = \left(\frac{33 \times 100}{132}\right) = 25$.

questions.question_text: The price of an article was increased by r%. Later the new price was decreased by r%. If the latest price was ₹ 1, then the original price was
questions.solution:
Let the original price be ₹ $x$.
$\therefore (100 - r)% of (100 + r)% of $x = 1$
$\Rightarrow \left(\frac{100 - r}{100}\right) \times \left(\frac{100 + r}{100}\right) \times x = 1$
$\Rightarrow x = \frac{100 \times 100}{(100 - r)(100 + r)} = \frac{10000}{(10000 - r^2)}$.

questions.question_text: In a factory, producing parts for an automobile, the parts manufactured on the shop floor are required to go through three quality checks, each conducted after a specific part of the processing on the raw material is completed. Only parts that are not rejected at one stage are put through the subsequent stages of production and testing. If average rejection rates at these testing machines during a month are 10%, 5% and 2% respectively, then what is the effective rejection rate for the whole plant?
questions.solution:
Let the number of parts before the quality checks be 100.
Then, number of parts passed after quality checks
= (100 - 2)% of (100 - 5)% of (100 - 10)% of 100
= 98% of 95% of 90% of 100
= $\left(\frac{98}{100} \times \frac{95}{100} \times \frac{90}{100} \times 100\right) = 83.79$.
$\therefore$ Effective rejection rate = (100 - 83.79)% = 16.21%.

questions.question_text: Peter could save 10% of his income. But two years later when his income is increased by 20%, he could save the same amount only as before. By how much percent has his expenditure increased?
questions.solution:
Let original income = ₹ 100. Then, saving = ₹ 10 and expenditure = ₹ 90.
New income = ₹ 120, New saving = ₹ 10. New expenditure = ₹ (120 - 10) = ₹ 110.
Increase in expenditure = ₹ (110 - 90) = ₹ 20.
$\therefore$ Increase % = $\left(\frac{20}{90} \times 100\right)\% = 22\frac{2}{9}\%$.

questions.question_text: A man spends 80% of his income. With an increase in the cost of living, his expenditure increases by $37\frac{1}{2}\%$ and his income increases by $16\frac{2}{3}\%$. His present percent savings are
questions.solution:
Let original income = ₹ 100. Then, saving = ₹ 20 and expenditure = ₹ 80.
New income = ₹ $116\frac{2}{3} = ₹ \frac{350}{3}$.
New expenditure = $137\frac{1}{2}\%$ of ₹ 80
= ₹ $\left(\frac{275}{2} \times \frac{1}{100} \times 80\right) = ₹ 110$.
New saving = ₹ $\left(\frac{350}{3} - 110\right) = ₹ \frac{20}{3}$.
$\therefore$ Required percentage = $\left(\frac{20}{3} \times \frac{1}{350} \times 100\right)\%$
= $5\frac{5}{7}\%$.

questions.question_text: A person spends 75% of his income. If his income increases by 20% and expenses increase by 15%, his saving will increase by
questions.solution:
Let original income = ₹ 100. Then, saving = ₹ 25 and expenditure = ₹ 75.
New income = ₹ 120.
New expenditure = 115% of ₹ 75 = ₹ $\left(\frac{115\times75}{100}\right)$ = ₹ $\frac{345}{4}$.
New saving = ₹ $\left(120 - \frac{345}{4}\right)$ = ₹ $\frac{135}{4}$.
Increase in saving = ₹ $\left(\frac{135}{4} - 25\right)$ = ₹ $\frac{35}{4}$.
Percentage increase = $\left(\frac{35}{4}\times\frac{1}{25}\times100\right)$% = 35%.

questions.question_text: Madan pays income tax at the rate of 10%. If his income increased by 10% and his tax rate increases to 15%, his net income would increase by ₹ 350. What is Madan’s income?
questions.solution:
Let Madan’s income be ₹ x.
Then, Net income = (100 - 10)% of ₹ x = 90% of ₹ x = ₹ $\frac{9x}{10}$.
New net income = 85% of 110% of ₹ x
= ₹ $\left(\frac{85}{100}\times\frac{110}{100}\times x\right)$ = ₹ $\frac{187}{200}x$.
$\frac{187}{200}x - \frac{9x}{10} = 350$
$\frac{7x}{200} = 350$
x = $\left(\frac{350\times200}{7}\right)$ = 10000.

questions.question_text: Mr. X, a businessman had the income in the year 2010, such that he earned a profit of 20% on his investment in the business. In the year 2011, his investment was less by ₹ 5000 but still had the same income (Income = Investment + Profit) as that in 2010. Thus, the percent profit earned in 2011 increased by 6%. What was his investment in 2010?
In the expression $xy^2$, the values of both variables x and y are decreased by 20%. By this, the value of the expression is decreased by
questions.solution:
Let X and Y denote the new values of x and y respectively.
Then, X = 80% of x = $\frac{4x}{5}$; Y = 80% of y = $\frac{4y}{5}$.
XY$^2$ = $\left(\frac{4x}{5}\right)\left(\frac{4y}{5}\right)^2 = \frac{4x}{5}\times\frac{16y^2}{25} = \frac{64}{125}xy^2$.
Decrease in the value = $\left(xy^2 - \frac{64}{125}xy^2\right) = \frac{61}{125}xy^2$.
Decrease % = $\left(\frac{61xy^2}{125}\times\frac{1}{xy^2}\times100\right)$% = 48.8%.

questions.question_text: When water is changed into ice, its volume increases by 9%. If ice changes into water, the percentage decrease in volume is
questions.solution:
Let V denote the volume of 1 c.c. of water.
Then, volume of ice formed from it = 109% of V = $\frac{109}{100}V$.
When this ice changes into water, decrease in volume
= $\left(\frac{109}{100}V - V\right) = \frac{9}{100}V$.
Decrease% = $\left(\frac{9V}{100}\times\frac{100}{109V}\times100\right)$% = $\frac{900}{109}$% = 8 $\frac{28}{109}$%.

questions.question_text: If the price of a commodity is decreased by 20% and its consumption is increased by 20%, what will be the increase or decrease in the expenditure on the commodity?
questions.solution:
Let original consumption = 100 units and original price = ₹ 100 per unit.
Original expenditure = ₹ (100 $\times$ 100) = ₹ 10000.
New expenditure = ₹ (120 $\times$ 80) = ₹ 9600.
Decrease in expenditure = $\left(\frac{400}{10000}\times100\right)$% = 4%.

questions.question_text: Income tax is raised from 4 paise to 5 paise in a rupee but the revenue is increased by 10% only. Find the decrease percent in the amount taxed.
questions.solution:
Let the original taxed amount be ₹ x and new taxed amount be ₹ y.
Let original revenue be ₹ 100.
Then, 4% of x = 100 or x = $\left(\frac{100\times100}{4}\right)$ = ₹ 2500.
New revenue = 110% of ₹ 100 = ₹ 110.
Then, 5% of y = 110 or y = $\left(\frac{110\times100}{5}\right)$ = ₹ 2200.
Decrease in taxed amount = ₹ (2500 - 2200) = ₹ 300.
Decrease % = $\left(\frac{300}{2500}\times100\right)$% = 12%.

questions.question_text: The ticket for admission to an exhibition was ₹ 5 and it was later reduced by 20%. As a result, the sale proceeds of tickets increased by 44%. What was the percentage increase in number of visitors?
questions.solution:
Let the total original sale be ₹ 100. Then, original number of visitors = $\frac{100}{5}$ = 20.
Reduced price of ticket = 80% of ₹ 5 = ₹ 4. New sale = 144% of ₹ 100 = ₹ 144.
New number of visitors = $\frac{144}{4}$ = 36.
Increase % = $\left(\frac{16}{20}\times100\right)$% = 80%.

questions.question_text: The income of a broker remains unchanged though the rate of commission is increased from 4% to 5%. The percentage of slump in business is
questions.solution:
Suppose the business value changes from x to y.
4% of x = 5% of y $\Rightarrow \frac{4}{100}x = \frac{5}{100}y \Rightarrow y = \frac{4}{5}x$.
Change in business = $\left(x - \frac{4}{5}x\right) = \frac{x}{5}$.
Percentage slump = $\left(\frac{x}{5}\times\frac{1}{x}\times100\right)$% = 20%.

questions.question_text: By how much percent must a motorist increase his speed in order to reduce by 20%, the time taken to cover a certain distance?
questions.solution:
Let the distance be x km and the original time taken be y hours. Then,
Original speed = $\left(\frac{x}{y}\right)$ km/hr.
New speed = $\left(\frac{x}{80\%\text{ of } y}\right)$ km/hr = $\left(\frac{5x}{4y}\right)$ km/hr.
Increase in speed = $\left(\frac{5x}{4y} - \frac{x}{y}\right) = \frac{x}{4y}$.
Increase% = $\left(\frac{x}{4y}\times\frac{y}{x}\times100\right)$% = 25%.

questions.question_text: What percent decrease in salaries would exactly cancel out the 20 percent increase?
questions.solution:
Let original salary = ₹ 100. New salary = ₹ 120.
Decrease on 120 = 20.
Decrease on 100 = $\left(\frac{20}{120}\times100\right)$% = 16 $\frac{2}{3}$%.

questions.question_text: The price of an article is reduced by 25%. In order to retain the original price, the present price has to be increased by
questions.solution:
Let original price = ₹ 100. New price = ₹ 75.
Increase on 75 = 25. Increase on 100
= $\left(\frac{25}{75}\times100\right)$% = 33 $\frac{1}{3}$%.

questions.question_text: A number is increased by 20% and then again by 20%. By what percent should the increased number be reduced so as to get back the original number?
questions.solution:
Let original number = 100.
New number = 120% of 120% of 100
= $\left(\frac{120}{100}\times\frac{120}{100}\times100\right)$ = 144.
Decrease on 144 = 44. Decrease on 100
= $\left(\frac{44}{144}\times100\right)$% = 30 $\frac{5}{9}$%.

questions.question_text: A person’s salary is decreased by steps of 20%, 15% and 10%. Approximately by what percent should the reduced salary be increased so as to get back the original salary?
questions.solution:
Let original salary = ₹ 100.
New salary = 90% of 85% of 80% of ₹ 100
= ₹ $\left(\frac{90}{100}\times\frac{85}{100}\times\frac{80}{100}\times100\right)$ = ₹ $\frac{306}{5}$.
Increase on $\frac{306}{5}$ = $\left(100 - \frac{306}{5}\right) = \frac{194}{5}$.
Increase on 100 = $\left(\frac{194}{5}\times\frac{5}{306}\times100\right)$% = $\frac{9700}{153}$% = 63.39% $\approx$ 63%.

questions.question_text: In a fraction, if numerator is increased by 40% and denominator is increased by 80%, then what fraction of the original is the new fraction?
questions.solution:
Let the original fraction be $\frac{x}{y}$. Then, new fraction
= $\frac{140\%\text{ of } x}{180\%\text{ of } y} = \frac{140x}{180y} = \frac{7x}{9y}$.
New fraction / Original fraction = $\left(\frac{7x}{9y}\times\frac{y}{x}\right) = \frac{7}{9}$.

questions.question_text: If the numerator of a fraction is increased by 240% and the denominator of the fraction is decreased by 50%, the resultant fraction is $2\frac{5}{6}$. What is the original fraction?
questions.solution:
Let the original fraction be $\frac{x}{y}$.
Then, new fraction = $\frac{(100+240)\%\text{ of } x}{(100-50)\%\text{ of } y} = \frac{340\%\text{ of } x}{50\%\text{ of } y} = \frac{34x}{5y}$.
$\frac{34x}{5y} = 2\frac{5}{6} = \frac{17}{6} \Rightarrow \frac{x}{y} = \frac{17}{6}\times\frac{5}{34} = \frac{5}{12}$.

questions.question_text: The price of a certain item is increased by 15%. If a consumer wants to keep his expenditure on the item same as before, how much percent must he reduce his consumption of that item?
questions.solution:
Reduction in consumption = $\left[\frac{R}{(100+R)}\times100\right]$%
= $\left(\frac{15}{115}\times100\right)$%
= $\frac{300}{23}$% = 13 $\frac{1}{23}$%.

questions.question_text: If the price of oil is increased by 30%, then by how much percent a family should reduce its consumption so that the expenditure would remain the same?
questions.solution:
Decrease in consumption = $\left[\frac{R}{(100+R)}\times100\right]$%
= $\left(\frac{30}{130}\times100\right)$% = 23 $\frac{1}{13}$%.

questions.question_text: The price of wheat falls by 16%. By what percentage a person can increase the consumption of wheat so that his overall budget does not change?
questions.solution:
Increase in consumption = $\left[\frac{R}{(100-R)}\times100\right]$%
= $\left(\frac{16}{84}\times100\right)$%
= $\frac{400}{21}$% = 19.04% $\approx$ 19%.

questions.question_text: The price of oil is increased by 25%. If the expenditure is not allowed to increase, the ratio between the reduction in consumption and the original consumption is
questions.solution:
Let original consumption be 1 unit costing ₹ 100.
New cost = ₹ 125. New consumption = $\left(\frac{1}{125}\times100\right)$ = $\frac{4}{5}$ unit.
Reduction in consumption / Original consumption = $\left(\frac{1-\frac{4}{5}}{1}\right)$ = $\frac{1}{5}$, i.e., 1:5.

questions.question_text: The price of sugar per kg increased from ₹ 16 to ₹ 20. The percentage reduction in the use of sugar so that the expenditure does not increase, should be

The price of sugar increases by 32%. A family reduces its consumption so that the expenditure of the sugar is up only by 10%. If the total consumption of the sugar before the price rise was 10 kg per month, then the consumption of sugar per month at present (in kg) is

Prices register an increase of 10% on foodgrains and 15% on other items of expenditure. If the ratio of an employee’s expenditure on foodgrains and other items be 2: 5, by how much should his salary be increased in order that he may maintain the same level of consumption as before, his present salary being ₹ 2590?

In the year 2010, 5000 students were admitted in a college. It is found that the number of students admitted is constantly increasing by 24 percent per year. How many students will be admitted in the college in the year 2012?
The salary of an employee increases consistently by 50% every year. If his salary today is ₹ 10000, what will be the salary after another 4 years?
A district has 64000 inhabitants. If the population increases at the rate of $2\frac{1}{2}\%$ per annum, then the number of inhabitants at the end of 3 years will be
If inflation increases at a rate of 8% p.a., what will a ₹ 20 article cost at the end of two years?
The population of a town is $4.2 \times 10^6$. If the population increases by 75 per 1000 per annum, then what will be the population after 2 years?
The population of a town is 8500. It increases by 20% in the first year and by another 25% in the second year. What would be the population of the town after 2 years?
The population of a town was 1,60,000 three years ago. If it increased by 3%, 2.5% and 5% respectively in the last three years, then the present population is

A merchant invests a certain sum and his annual gain percent is 25. If at the end of the third year, his capital is ₹ 10000, then the amount invested by him is equal to
