The value of a machine depreciates at the rate of 12 percent per annum. It was purchased three years ago. Its present value is ₹ 29644.032. What was the purchase price of the machine?

The value of a fixed asset depreciates at the rate of 10% of the value at the beginning of each year. If the value of the asset, two years ago, was ₹ 12000 more than the value of the asset one year ago, then find the present value of the asset, given that the asset was bought two years ago.
A tree increases annually by $\frac{1}{8}$ of its height. By how much will it increase after $2\frac{1}{2}$ years if it stands today 8 m high?
The population of a town is 189000. It decreases by 8% in the first year and increases by 5% in the second year. What is the population of the town at the end of 2 years?

Ashish started a business with an initial investment of ₹ 500000. In the first year, he incurred a loss of 4%. However, during the second year, he earned a profit of 5% which in the third year rose to 10%. Calculate his net profit for the entire period of three years.
The present value of an optical instrument is ₹ 20000. If its value will depreciate 5% in the first year, 4% in the second year and 2% in the third year, what will be its value after 3 years?
questions.solution:
Value after 3 years = ₹ $\left[20000\left(1-\frac{5}{100}\right)\left(1-\frac{4}{100}\right)\left(1-\frac{2}{100}\right)\right]$
= ₹ $\left(20000\times\frac{95}{100}\times\frac{96}{100}\times\frac{98}{100}\right)$ = ₹ 17875.2

questions.question_text: The population of a variety of tiny bush in an experimental field increased by 10% in the first year, increased by 8% in the second year but decreased by 10% in the third year. If the present number of bushes in the experimental field is 26730, then the number of bushes in the beginning was
questions.solution:
Number of bushes in the beginning
= $\frac{26730}{\left(1+\frac{10}{100}\right)\left(1+\frac{8}{100}\right)\left(1-\frac{10}{100}\right)}$
= $\left(26730\times\frac{10}{11}\times\frac{25}{27}\times\frac{10}{9}\right) = 25000$

questions.question_text: The production of a company has ups and downs every year. The production increases for two consecutive years consistently by 15% and in the third year it decreases by 10%. Again in the next two years it increases by 15% each year and decreases by 10% in the third year. If we start counting from the year 2008, approximately what will be the effect on production of the company in 2012?
questions.solution:
Let the production in 2008 be 100 units. Then,
Production in 2012 = $100\times\left(1+\frac{15}{100}\right)\left(1-\frac{10}{100}\right)\left(1+\frac{15}{100}\right)$
= $\left(100\times\frac{23}{20}\times\frac{9}{10}\times\frac{23}{20}\right) = 136.88$.
Increase in production = $(136.88-100)\% = 36.88\% \approx 37\%$.

questions.question_text: The present population of a country estimated to be 10 crores is expected to increase to 13.31 crores during the next three years. The uniform rate of growth is
questions.solution:
10 crores $\times\left(1+\frac{R}{100}\right)^3 = 13.31$ crores.
$\left(1+\frac{R}{100}\right)^3 = \frac{13.31}{10} = \frac{1331}{1000} = \left(\frac{11}{10}\right)^3$.
So, $1+\frac{R}{100} = \frac{11}{10} \Leftrightarrow \frac{R}{100} = \frac{11}{10}-1 \Leftrightarrow \frac{R}{100} = \frac{1}{10} \Leftrightarrow R = 10$.

questions.question_text: The price of a commodity which was ₹ 250 three years ago is ₹ 2000 now. The annual rate of increase in the price is
questions.solution:
$250\left(1+\frac{R}{100}\right)^3 = 2000 \Leftrightarrow \left(1+\frac{R}{100}\right)^3 = \frac{2000}{250} = 8 = (2)^3$
$\Leftrightarrow 1+\frac{R}{100} = 2 \Leftrightarrow \frac{R}{100} = 1 \Leftrightarrow R = 100$.

questions.question_text: Raju’s factory kept increasing its output by the same percentage every year. Find the percentage if it is known that his output is doubled after two years.
questions.solution:
Let the initial output be $x$ and let the rate of increase be $R\%$ per year.
Then, $x\left(1+\frac{R}{100}\right)^2 = 2x \Leftrightarrow \left(1+\frac{R}{100}\right)^2 = 2 = (\sqrt{2})^2$
$\Leftrightarrow 1+\frac{R}{100} = \sqrt{2} \Leftrightarrow R = 100(\sqrt{2}-1)$.

questions.question_text: The population of a colony was 3600 three years back. It is 4800 right now. What will be the population three years down the line, if the rate of growth of population has been constant over the years and has been compounding annually?
questions.solution:
Let the rate of growth be $R\%$ per annum.
Then, $3600\left(1+\frac{R}{100}\right)^3 = 4800 \Leftrightarrow \left(1+\frac{R}{100}\right)^3 = \frac{4800}{3600} = \frac{4}{3}$.
Population after 3 years = $4800\left(1+\frac{R}{100}\right)^3$
= $4800\times\frac{4}{3} = 6400$.

questions.question_text: The value of a flat worth ₹ 500000 is depreciating at the rate of 10% p.a. In how many years will its value be reduced to ₹ 364500?
questions.solution:
Let the required time be $n$ years.
Then, $500000\left(1-\frac{10}{100}\right)^n = 364500$
$\left(\frac{9}{10}\right)^n = \frac{364500}{500000} = \frac{729}{1000}$
$\left(\frac{9}{10}\right)^n = \left(\frac{9}{10}\right)^3 \Leftrightarrow n = 3$.

questions.question_text: A building worth ₹ 1,33,100 is constructed on land worth ₹ 72,900. After how many years will the value of both be the same if land appreciates at 10% p.a. and building depreciates at 10% p.a.?
questions.solution:
Let the required time be $n$ years. Then,
$72900\times\left(1+\frac{10}{100}\right)^n = 133100\times\left(1-\frac{10}{100}\right)^n$
$\left(\frac{11}{10}\right)^n\times\left(\frac{9}{10}\right)^n = \frac{133100}{72900} = \frac{1331}{729}$
$\left(\frac{99}{100}\right)^n = \left(\frac{11}{9}\right)^3 \Leftrightarrow n = 3$.

questions.question_text: Given that carbon-14 decays at a constant rate in such a way that it reduces to 50% in 5568 years, find the age of an old wooden piece in which the carbon is only 12.5% of the original.
questions.solution:
Let $T$ denote the required number of 5568 year-intervals.
Then, $x\left(1-\frac{50}{100}\right)^T = 12.5\%$ of $x \Leftrightarrow \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^T = \frac{12.5}{100} = \frac{1}{8} = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^3 \Leftrightarrow T = 3$.

questions.question_text: The population of a town increases 4% annually but is decreased by emigration annually to the extent of (1/2)%. What will be the increase percent in 3 years?
questions.solution:
Let original population = 100.
Population after 3 years
= $100\times\left(1+\frac{3}{100}\right)^3 = 100\times\frac{207}{200}\times\frac{207}{200}\times\frac{207}{200} = 110.87$.
Increase = $(110.87-100)\% = 10.87\% \approx 10.8\%$.

questions.question_text: The current birth rate per thousand is 32, whereas the corresponding death rate is 11 per thousand. The net growth rate in terms of population increase in percent is given by
questions.solution:
Net growth on 1000 = $(32-11) = 21$. Net growth on 100
= $\left(\frac{21}{1000}\times100\right)\% = 2.1\%$.

questions.passage_text: Mr. Jones’ total annual gross salary, which was ₹ 10 lakhs per year in 2007, was reduced by 10% in 2008. In 2007 his family expenditure for food items was 40% of the total annual gross salary. The prices of average food items increased by 5% between 2007 and 2008. Assuming that the family consumed the same amount of food in 2008, the
questions.question_text: percentage expenditure on food items, calculated on total annual gross salary in 2008, is
questions.solution:
Annual gross salary in 2007 = ₹ 1000000.
Expenditure on food items in 2007 = $40\%$ of ₹ 1000000 = ₹ 400000.
Annual gross salary in 2008 = $90\%$ of ₹ 1000000 = ₹ 900000.
Expenditure on food items in 2008 = $105\%$ of ₹ 400000 = ₹ 420000.
Required percentage = $\left(\frac{420000}{900000}\times100\right)\% = \frac{140}{3}\% = 46.6\% \approx 47\%$.

questions.question_text: A man’s income is increased by ₹ 1200 and at the same time, the rate of tax to be paid is reduced from 12% to 10%. He now pays the same amount of tax as before. What is his increased income if 20% of his income is exempted from tax in both cases?
questions.solution:
Let the man's original income be ₹ $x$.
Then, original tax paid = ₹ $[12\%$ of $(80\%$ of $x)]$
= ₹ $\left(\frac{12}{100}\times\frac{80}{100}\times x\right) = ₹ \frac{12x}{125}$.
New income = ₹ $(x+1200)$.
New tax paid = ₹ $[10\%$ of $\{80\%$ of $(x+1200)\}]$
= ₹ $\left[\frac{10}{100}\times\frac{80}{100}\times(x+1200)\right] = ₹ \left[\frac{2}{25}(x+1200)\right] = ₹ \left(\frac{2x}{25}+96\right)$.
$\frac{12x}{125} = \frac{2x}{25}+96 \Leftrightarrow \frac{12x-10x}{125} = 96 \Leftrightarrow x = \frac{96\times125}{2} = 6000$.
Hence, increased income = ₹ $(6000+1200) = ₹ 7200$.

questions.question_text: If A’s income is 10% more than B’s, how much percentage is B’s income less than A’s?
questions.solution:
B's income is less than A's by
$\left[\frac{10}{(100+10)}\times100\right]\%$, i.e., $9\frac{1}{11}\%$.

questions.question_text: A’s income is 25% more than B’s income. B’s income in terms of A’s income is
questions.solution:
A = $125\%$ of B
$\Rightarrow A = \frac{125}{100}B \Rightarrow B = \frac{100}{125}A = \frac{4}{5}A = 80\%$ of A.

questions.question_text: If A’s wage with respect to B’s wage is 20% more, then how much percent is B’s wage less with respect to A’s wage?
questions.solution:
B's wage is less than A's by
$\left[\frac{20}{(100+20)}\times100\right]\%$, i.e., $16\frac{2}{3}\%$.

questions.question_text: If A’s income is 50% less than that of B, then B’s income is what percent more than that of A?
questions.solution: B's income is more than A's by $\left[\frac{50}{(100-50)}\times100\right]\%$, i.e., $100\%$.

questions.question_text: If x is 25% less than y, then what percent is y more than x?
questions.solution: $y$ is more than $x$ by $\left[\frac{25}{(100-25)}\times100\right]\%$, i.e., $33\frac{1}{3}\%$.

questions.question_text: p is six times as large as q. The percent that q is less than p, is
questions.solution:
$p = 6q$. So, $q$ is less than $p$ by $5q$.
Required percentage
= $\left(\frac{5q}{p}\times100\right)\% = \left(\frac{5q}{6q}\times100\right)\% = 83\frac{1}{3}\%$.

questions.question_text: Two numbers are respectively 20% and 25% lower than a third number. By how much percentage is the second number lower than the first?
questions.solution:
Let the third number be $x$.
Then, first number $= 80\%$ of $x = \frac{80}{100}\times x = \frac{4}{5}x$.
Second number $= 75\%$ of $x = \frac{75}{100}\times x = \frac{3}{4}x$.
Difference $= \frac{4}{5}x - \frac{3}{4}x = \frac{x}{20}$.
$\therefore$ Required percentage $= \left(\frac{x}{20}\times\frac{5}{4x}\times100\right)\% = \frac{25}{4}\% = 6\frac{1}{4}\%$.

questions.question_text: Two numbers are respectively $12\frac{1}{2}$% and 25% more than a third number. The first number as a percentage of the second number is
questions.solution:
Let third number be $x$.
Then, first number $= 112\frac{1}{2}\%$ of $x = \frac{9}{8}x$;
Second number $= 125\%$ of $x = \frac{5}{4}x$.
$\therefore$ Required percentage $= \left(\frac{9x}{8}\times\frac{4}{5x}\times100\right)\% = 90\%$.

questions.question_text: A’s salary is 40% of B’s salary which is 25% of C’s salary. What percentage of C’s salary is A’s salary?
Peter earned 40% more money than Albert. Albert earned 20% less than Michael. Peter earned more than Michael by
questions.solution:
$P = \left(\frac{140}{100}\times\frac{140}{100}\times\frac{80}{100}\right)M$
$= \left(\frac{140}{100}\times\frac{80}{100}\times100\right)\%$ of $M = 112\%$ of $M$.
$\therefore$ Peter earned $12\%$ more than Michael

questions.question_text: Fresh fruit contains 68% water and dry fruit contains 20% water. How much dry fruit can be obtained from 100 kg of fresh fruits?
questions.solution:
Quantity of pulp in $100$ kg of fresh fruits $= (100 - 68)\%$ of $100$ kg $= 32$ kg.
Let the quantity of dry fruit obtained be $x$ kg.
Then, $(100 - 20)\%$ of $x = 32$
$\Leftrightarrow \frac{80}{100}\times x = 32 \Leftrightarrow x = \left(\frac{32\times100}{80}\right) = 40$.

questions.question_text: A large watermelon weighs 20 kg with 96% of its weight being water. It is allowed to stand in the sun and some of the water evaporates so that only 95% of its weight is water. Its reduced weight will be
questions.solution:
Let the reduced weight be $x$ kg.
Clearly, the quantity of pulp remains the same in both the cases.
So, $(100 - 96)\%$ of $20$ kg $= (100 - 95)\%$ of $x$ kg
$\Leftrightarrow 4\%$ of $20$ kg $= 5\%$ of $x$ kg $\Leftrightarrow x = \left(\frac{4\times20}{5}\right)$ kg $= 16$ kg.

questions.question_text: Fresh grapes contain 80 percent water while dry grapes contain 10 percent water. If the weight of dry grapes is 250 kg what was its total weight when it was fresh?
questions.solution:
Quantity of pulp in fresh grapes $= $ Quantity of pulp in dry grapes
$= (100 - 10)\%$ of $250$ kg $= 90\%$ of $250$ kg $= 225$ kg.
Let the total weight of fresh grapes be $x$ kg.
Then, $(100 - 80)\%$ of $x = 225 \Leftrightarrow 20\%$ of $x = 225 \Leftrightarrow \frac{x}{5} = 225$
$\Rightarrow x = 225 \times 5 = 1125$.
Hence, total weight of fresh grapes $= 1125$ kg.

questions.question_text: An alloy of gold and silver weighs 50 g. It contains 80% gold. How much gold should be added to the alloy so that percentage of gold is increased to 90?
questions.solution:
Quantity of gold in the alloy $= 80\%$ of $50$ g
$= \frac{80}{100}\times 50$ g $= 40$ g.
Let $x$ g of gold be added.
Then, $\frac{40+x}{50+x} = \frac{90}{100} = \frac{9}{10} \Rightarrow 400 + 10x = 450 + 9x \Rightarrow x = 50$.

questions.question_text: In a mixture of milk and water, the proportion of water by weight was 75%. If in the 60 gms mixture 15 gm of water was added, what would be the percentage of water?
questions.solution:
Weight of water in $60$ gms mixture $= 75\%$ of $60$ gm
$= \frac{75}{100}\times 60$ gm $= 45$ gm.
Weight of water in $75$ gms mixture $= (45 + 15)$ gm $= 60$ gm.
$\therefore$ Required percentage $= \left(\frac{60}{75}\times100\right)\% = 80\%$.

questions.question_text: One litre of water is evaporated from a 6 litre solution containing 4% sugar. The percentage of sugar in the remaining solution is
questions.solution:
Quantity of sugar $= \left(\frac{4}{100}\times 6\right)$ kg $= 0.24$ kg.
$\therefore$ New percentage $= \left(\frac{0.24}{5}\times100\right)\% = 4\frac{4}{5}\%$.

questions.question_text: The quantity of water (in ml) needed to reduce 9 ml shaving lotion containing 50% alcohol to a lotion containing 30% alcohol, is
questions.solution:
Quantity of alcohol in $9$ ml lotion $= \left(\frac{50}{100}\times 9\right)$ ml $= 4.5$ ml.
Let the water to be added be $x$ ml.
Then, $\frac{4.5}{9+x} = \frac{30}{100} \Leftrightarrow 270 + 30x = 450 \Leftrightarrow x = 6$ ml.

questions.question_text: 1 litre of water is added to 5 litres of alcohol-water solution containing 40% alcohol strength. The strength of alcohol in the new solution will be
questions.solution:
Quantity of alcohol $= \left(\frac{40}{100}\times 5\right)$ litres $= 2$ litres.
$\therefore$ New strength $= \left(\frac{2}{6}\times100\right)\% = 33\frac{1}{3}\%$.

questions.question_text: To strengthen 400 ml of 15% alcohol solution to 32% alcohol solution, a pharmacist would need additional pure alcohol amounting to
questions.solution:
Quantity of alcohol in $400$ ml solution $= 15\%$ of $400$ ml $= 60$ ml.
Let $x$ ml of alcohol be added.
Then, $\frac{60+x}{400+x} = \frac{32}{100} \Rightarrow 6000 + 100x = 12800 + 32x$
$\Rightarrow 68x = 6800 \Rightarrow x = 100$.

questions.question_text: 6 c.c. of a 20% solution of alcohol in water is mixed with 4 c.c. of a 60% solution of alcohol in water. The alcoholic strength of the mixture is
questions.solution:
Quantity of alcohol in $10$ c.c. solution
$= (20\%$ of $6 + 60\%$ of $4)$ c.c. $= \left(\frac{20}{100}\times 6 + \frac{60}{100}\times 4\right)$ c.c.
$= (1.2 + 2.4)$ c.c. $= 3.6$ c.c.
$\therefore$ Required strength $= \left(\frac{3.6}{10}\times100\right)\% = 36\%$.

questions.question_text: One type of liquid contains 20% water and the second type of liquid contains 35% of water. A glass is filled with 10 parts of first liquid and 4 parts of second liquid. The percentage of water in the new mixture in the glass is
questions.solution:
Required percentage $= \left(\frac{20\% \text{ of } 10 + 35\% \text{ of } 4}{10+4}\times100\right)\%$
$= \left(\frac{3.4}{14}\times100\right)\% = 24\frac{2}{7}\%$.

questions.question_text: In some quantity of ghee, 60% is pure ghee and 40% is vanaspati. If 10 kg of pure ghee is added, then the strength of vanaspati ghee becomes 20%. The original quantity was
questions.solution:
Let the original quantity be $x$ kg. Vanaspati ghee in $x$ kg
$= \left(\frac{40}{100}x\right)$ kg $= \frac{2}{5}x$ kg.
Now, $\frac{2x/5}{x+10} = \frac{20}{100} \Leftrightarrow \frac{2x}{5x+50} = \frac{1}{5} \Leftrightarrow 5x = 50 \Leftrightarrow x = 10$.

questions.question_text: From 5 litres of a 20% solution of alcohol in water, 2 litres of solution is taken out and 2 litres of water is added to it. Find the strength of alcohol in the new solution.
questions.solution:
Quantity of alcohol in $5$ litres solution $= 20\%$ of $5$ litres $= 1$ litres.
Quantity of alcohol removed $= 20\%$ of $2$ litres $= 400$ ml.
Quantity of alcohol in new solution $= (1000 - 400)$ ml $= 600$ ml.
$\therefore$ Strength of alcohol in new solution
$= \left(\frac{600}{5000}\times100\right)\% = 12\%$.

questions.question_text: After 30 kg of water had been evaporated from a solution of salt and water, which had 15% salt, the remaining solution had 20% salt. The weight of the original solution was
questions.solution:
Let the weight of the original solution be $x$ kg.
Weight of salt in $x$ kg of solution $= 15\%$ of $x$ kg $= \frac{3}{20}x$ kg.
$\therefore \frac{3x/20}{x-30} = \frac{20}{100} \Rightarrow \frac{3x}{20(x-30)} = \frac{1}{5}$
$\Rightarrow 15x = 20(x - 30) \Rightarrow 15x = 20x - 600$
$\Rightarrow 5x = 600 \Rightarrow x = 120$.

questions.question_text: 85% and 92% alcoholic solutions are mixed to get 35 litres of an 89% alcoholic solution. How many litres of each solution are there in the new mixture?
questions.option_a: 10 of the first and 25 of the second
questions.option_b: 20 of the first and 15 of the second
questions.option_c: 15 of the first and 20 of the second
questions.option_d: 12 of the first and 23 of the second
questions.solution:
Let $x$ litres of the first and $(35 - x)$ litres of the second solution be mixed together.
Then, $85\%$ of $x + 92\%$ of $(35 - x) = 89\%$ of $35$
$\Rightarrow 85x + 92 (35 - x) = 89 \times 35$
$\Rightarrow 85x + 3220 - 92x = 3115 \Rightarrow 7x = 105 \Rightarrow x = 15$.
Hence, quantity of first solution $= 15$ litres ; quantity of second solution $= 20$ litres.

questions.question_text: How many litres of a 30% alcohol solution should be added to 40 litres of a 60% alcohol solution to prepare a 50% solution?
questions.solution:
Let $x$ litres of $30\%$ alcohol solution be added.
Then, $30\%$ of $x + 60\%$ of $40 = 50\%$ of $(x + 40)$
$\Rightarrow 30x + 60 \times 40 = 50 (x + 40)$
$\Rightarrow 30x + 2400 = 50x + 2000$
$\Rightarrow 20x = 400$
$\Rightarrow x = 20$.

questions.question_text: A 27 quartz capacity car radiator is filled with 18% alcohol solution. How many quartz be drained and then be replaced by a 90% alcohol solution for resulting solution to contain 42% alcohol?
questions.solution:
Let $x$ quartz of the first solution be drained and replaced
by $x$ quartz of the second solution.
Then, $18\%$ of $(27 - x) + 90\%$ of $x = 42\%$ of $27$
$\Rightarrow 18(27 - x) + 90x = 42 \times 27$
$\Rightarrow 486 - 18x + 90x = 1134$
$\Rightarrow 72x = 648$
$\Rightarrow x = 9$.

questions.question_text: Two vessels contain equal quantities of 40% alcohol. Sachin changed the concentration of the first vessel to 50% by adding extra quantity of pure alcohol. Vivek changed the concentration of the second vessel to 50% replacing a certain quantity of the solution with pure alcohol. By what percentage is the quantity of alcohol added by Sachin more/less than that replaced by Vivek?
questions.solution:
Let each vessel contain $100$ litres of $40\%$ alcohol.
Suppose Sachin added $x$ litres of pure alcohol.
Then, $\frac{40 + x}{100 + x} = \frac{50}{100} \Rightarrow 80 + 2x = 100 + x \Rightarrow x = 20$.
Suppose Vivek replaced $y$ litres.
Then, alcohol in $y$ litres $= 40\%$ of $y = \frac{2}{5}$ litres.
$\frac{40 - \frac{2y}{5}}{100} = \frac{50}{100} \Rightarrow 80 + \frac{6y}{5} = 100$
$\Rightarrow y = \frac{20 \times 5}{6} = \frac{50}{3}$.
Required percentage $= \left[ \frac{20 - \frac{50}{3}}{\frac{50}{3}} \right] \times 100 \%$
$= \left( \frac{10}{3} \times \frac{3}{50} \times 100 \right) \% = 20\%$.

questions.question_text: From a container having pure milk, 20% is replaced by water and the process is repeated thrice. At the end of the third operation, the milk is
questions.solution:
Let total quantity of original milk $= 1000$ gm.
Milk after first operation $= 80\%$ of $1000 = 800$ gm.
Milk after second operation $= 80\%$ of $800 = 640$ gm.
Milk after third operation $= 80\%$ of $640 = 512$ gm.
$\therefore$ Strength of final mixture $= 51.2\%$.

questions.question_text: An empty fuel tank of a car was filled with A type petrol. When the tank was half-empty, it was filled with B type petrol. Again when the tank was half-empty, it was filled with A type petrol. When the tank was half-empty again, it was filled with B type petrol. What is the percentage of A type petrol at present in the tank?
questions.solution:
Let the capacity of the tank be $100$ litres. Then,
Initially: A type petrol $= 100$ litres.
After first operation:
A type petrol $= \left( \frac{100}{2} \right) = 50$ litres ; B type petrol $= 50$
litres.
After second operation:
A type petrol $= \left( \frac{50}{2} + 50 \right) = 75$ litres ;

questions.question_text: A bag contains 600 coins of 25 p denomination and 1200 coins of 50 p denomination. If 12% of 25 p coins and 24% of 50 p coins are removed, the percentage of money removed from the bag is nearly
questions.solution:
Total money $= ₹ \left( 600 \times \frac{25}{100} + 1200 \times \frac{50}{100} \right) = ₹ 750$.
$25$ paise coins removed $= \left( \frac{24}{100} \times 1200 \right) = 72$. $50$ paise coins
removed $= \left( \frac{24}{100} \times 1200 \right) = 288$.
Money removed $= ₹ \left( 72 \times \frac{25}{100} + 288 \times \frac{50}{100} \right) = ₹ 162$.
$\therefore$ Required percentage $= \left( \frac{162}{750} \times 100 \right) \% = 21.6\%$.

questions.question_text: The price of rice is reduced by 2%. How many kilograms of rice can now be bought for the money which was sufficient to buy 49 kg of rice earlier?
questions.solution:
Let the original price be $₹ 100$ per kg.
Money required to buy $49$ kg of rice $= ₹ (100 \times 49)$
$= ₹ 4900$.
New price $= ₹ 98$ per kg.
$\therefore$ Quantity of rice bought $= \left( \frac{4900}{98} \right)$ kg $= 50$ kg.

questions.question_text: If the price of erasers goes down by 25%, a man can buy 2 more erasers for a rupee. How many erasers are available for a rupee?
questions.solution:
Let the original price of an eraser be $₹ x$.
Reduced price $= 75\%$ of $₹ x = ₹ \frac{3x}{4}$.
$\therefore \frac{1}{\frac{3x}{4}} - \frac{1}{x} = 2$
$\Rightarrow \frac{4}{3x} - \frac{1}{x} = 2 \Rightarrow \frac{1}{3x} = 2 \Rightarrow 6x = 1 \Rightarrow x = \frac{1}{6}$.
Hence, number of erasers available for a rupee $= 6$.

questions.question_text: A reduction of 21% in the price of wheat enables a person to buy 10.5 kg more for ₹ 100. What is the reduced price per kg?
questions.solution:
Let original price $= ₹ x$ per kg. Reduced price $= ₹ \left( \frac{79x}{100} \right)$
per kg.
$\therefore \frac{100}{79x} - \frac{100}{x} = 10.5$
$\Leftrightarrow 10000 - 7900 = 10.5 \times 79x \Leftrightarrow x = \frac{2100}{10.5 \times 79}$.
$\therefore$ Reduced price $= ₹ \left( \frac{79}{100} \times \frac{2100}{10.5 \times 79} \right)$ per kg
$= ₹ 2$ per kg.

questions.question_text: Due to an increase of 30% in the price of eggs, 3 eggs less are available for ₹ 7.80. The present rate of eggs per dozen is
questions.solution:
Let the original price per egg be $₹ x$. Then, increased price
$= ₹ \left( \frac{130}{100}x \right)$.
$\therefore \frac{7.80}{x} - \frac{7.80}{\frac{130}{100}x} = 3$
$\Leftrightarrow 1014 - 780 = 3 \times 130x \Rightarrow 390x = 234 \Leftrightarrow x = 0.6$.
So, present price per dozen $= ₹ \left( 12 \times \frac{130}{100} \times 0.6 \right) = ₹ 9.36$.

questions.question_text: The price of sugar having gone down by 10%, Sharad can buy 6.2 kg more for ₹ 279. The difference between the original and the reduced price (per kg) is
questions.solution:
Let original price $= ₹ x$ per kg. Reduced price
$= ₹ \left( \frac{90x}{100} \right)$ per kg.
$\therefore \frac{279}{\frac{90x}{100}} - \frac{279}{x} = 6.2 \Leftrightarrow \frac{27900}{90x} - \frac{279}{x} = 6.2$
$\Leftrightarrow 27900 - 25110 = 6.2 \times 90x$
$\Leftrightarrow 558x = 2790 \Leftrightarrow x = 5$.
$\therefore$ Required difference $= 10\%$ of $₹ 5 = ₹ 0.50$.

questions.question_text: If the price of sugar falls by $1\frac{1}{2}\%$, a person can buy 9 kg more of sugar for ₹ 1260 than before. If the price had risen by $1\frac{1}{2}\%$, how much sugar would he have bought for the same sum?
questions.solution:
Let the original price of sugar be $₹ x$ per kg.
Then, reduced price $= 97 \frac{1}{2} \%$ of $₹ x$
$= ₹ \left( \frac{195}{2 \times 100} \times x \right) = ₹ \frac{39x}{40}$.
$\therefore \frac{1260}{\frac{39x}{40}} - \frac{1260}{x} = 9 \Leftrightarrow \frac{16800}{13x} - \frac{1260}{x} = 9$
$\Leftrightarrow 13x = \frac{420}{9} \Rightarrow x = \frac{140}{39}$.
Increased price $= 112 \frac{1}{2} \%$ of $₹ \frac{140}{39}$
$= ₹ \left( \frac{225}{2 \times 100} \times \frac{140}{39} \right) = ₹ \frac{105}{26}$.
$\therefore$ Quantity of sugar bought for $₹ 1260$
$= \left( 1260 \times \frac{26}{105} \right)$ kg $= 312$ kg.

questions.question_text: In a survey of a city, it was found that 90 percent of the people in the city own a refrigerator and 15 percent own a washing machine. If everybody owns at least one appliance, what percentage owns both?
questions.solution:
$n (A) = 90, n (B) = 15, n (A \cup B) = 100$.
So, $n (A \cap B) = n (A) + n (B) - n (A \cup B)$
$= 90 + 15 - 100 = 5$.
$\therefore$ Percentage of people who own both $= 5\%$.

questions.question_text: In an examination, 34% of the students failed in Mathematics and 42% failed in English. If 20% of the students failed in both the subjects, then the percentage of students who passed in both the subjects was
questions.solution:
$n (A) = 34, n (B) = 42, n (A \cap B) = 20$.
So, $n (A \cup B) = n (A) + n (B) - n (A \cap B)$
$= 34 + 42 - 20 = 56$.
$\therefore$ Percentage failed in either or both the subjects $= 56$.
Hence, percentage passed $= (100 - 56)\% = 44\%$.

questions.question_text: 40% of the people read newspaper X, 50% read newspaper Y and 10% read both the papers. What percentage of the people read neither newspaper?
questions.solution:
$n (A) = 40, n (B) = 50, n (A \cap B) = 10$.
$n (A \cup B) = n (A) + n (B) - n (A \cap B)$
$= 40 + 50 - 10 = 80$.
$\therefore$ Percentage reading either or both newspapers $= 80\%$.
Hence, percentage reading neither newspaper
$= (100 - 80)\% = 20\%$.

questions.question_text: Out of 450 students of a school, 325 play football, 175 play cricket and 50 neither play football nor cricket. How many students play both football and cricket?
In a hotel, 60% had vegetarian lunch while 30% had non-vegetarian lunch and 15% had both types of lunch. If 96 people were present, how many did not eat either type of lunch?
questions.solution:
$n (A) = \left( \frac{60}{100} \times 96 \right) = \frac{288}{5}, n (B)$
$= \left( \frac{30}{100} \times 96 \right) = \frac{144}{5}, n (A \cap B) = \left( \frac{15}{100} \times 96 \right) = \frac{72}{5}$.
$\therefore n (A \cup B) = n (A) + n (B) - n (A \cap B)$
$= \frac{288}{5} + \frac{144}{5} - \frac{72}{5} = \frac{360}{5} = 72$.
So, people who had either or both types of lunch $= 72$.
Hence, people who had neither type of lunch $= (96 - 72) = 24$.

questions.question_text: There are 600 boys in a hostel. Each plays either hockey or football or both. If 75% play hockey and 45% play football, how many play both?
questions.solution:
$n (A) = \left( \frac{75}{100} \times 600 \right) = 450, n (B) = \left( \frac{45}{100} \times 600 \right) = 270$,
$n (A \cup B) = 600$.
$\therefore n (A \cap B) = n (A) + n (B) - n (A \cup B)$
$= (450 + 270 - 600) = 120$.

questions.question_text: In a certain office, 72% of the workers prefer tea and 44% prefer coffee. If each of them prefers tea or coffee and 40 like both, the total number of workers in the office is
questions.solution:
Let total number be $x$. Then,
$n (A) = \frac{72}{100}x = \frac{18x}{25}, n (B) = \frac{44}{100}x = \frac{11x}{25}$ and $n (A \cap B) = 40$.
$n (A \cup B) = n (A) + n (B) - n (A \cap B)$
$\Rightarrow \frac{18x}{25} + \frac{11x}{25} - 40 = x$
$\Rightarrow \frac{4x}{25} = 40 \Rightarrow x = 250$.

questions.question_text: In an examination, 30% and 35% students respectively failed in History and Geography while 27% students failed in both the subjects. If the number of students passing the examination is 248, find the total number of students who appeared in the examination.
questions.solution:
Percentage of failed candidates $= (30 + 35 - 27)\% = 38\%$.
Percentage of passed candidates $= (100 - 38)\% = 62\%$.
Let the total number of students appeared be $x$.
Then, $62\%$ of $x = 248 \Rightarrow x = \frac{248 \times 100}{62} = 400$.

questions.question_text: In an examination, 35% candidates failed in one subject and 42% failed in another subject while 15% failed in both the subjects. If 2500 candidates appeared at the examination, how many passed in either subject but not in both?
questions.solution:
Failed in 1st subject $= \left( \frac{35}{100} \times 2500 \right) = 875$.
Failed in 2nd subject $= \left( \frac{42}{100} \times 2500 \right) = 1050$.
Failed in both $= \left( \frac{15}{100} \times 2500 \right) = 375$.
Failed in 1st subject only $= (875 - 375) = 500$.
Failed in 2nd subject only $= (1050 - 375) = 675$.
$\therefore$ Passed in 2nd only + Passed in 1st only
$= (675 + 500) = 1175$.

questions.question_text: Arnav ordered 4 pairs of black socks and some additional pairs of blue socks. The price of the black socks per pair was twice that of the blue socks. When the order was filled, it was found that the number of pairs of the two colours had been interchanged. This increased the bill by 50%. The ratio of the number of pairs of black socks to the number of pairs of blue socks in the original order was
questions.solution:
Suppose he ordered $n$ pairs of blue socks.
Let the price of each pair of blue socks be ₹ $x$.
Then, price of each pair of black socks = ₹ $2x$.
Actual bill = ₹ $(4 	imes 2x + nx) = ₹ (8x + nx)$.
Bill made on interchange = ₹ $(2nx + 4x)$.
$\therefore 2nx + 4x = 150\%$ of $(8x + nx)$
$\Rightarrow 2nx + 4x = \frac{3}{2} (8x + nx)$
$\Rightarrow 2(2nx + 4x) = 3(8x + nx)$
$\Rightarrow nx = 16x \Rightarrow n = 16$.
Hence, required ratio = $4:16 = 1:4$.

questions.question_text: A man ordered a length of rope by telephone from his nearest hardware shop. But when a worker in the shop brought the rope, he found that the man on the telephone had miswritten the order by interchanging feet and inches. As a result of this, the length of rope received was only 30% of the length he had ordered. The length of the rope which the man ordered was between
questions.solution:
Suppose the man ordered $x$ feet $y$ inches of the rope. Since $x$ and $y$ represent inches in the miswritten and actual order respectively, so each one of $x$ and $y$ is less than $12$.
$[\because 1$ feet $= 12$ inches$]$
Actual order $= x$ feet $y$ inches $= (12x + y)$ inches.
Miswritten order $= y$ feet $x$ inches $= (12y + x)$ inches.
$\therefore (12y + x) = 30\%$ of $(12x + y) = \frac{3}{10} (12x + y)$
$\Rightarrow 10 (12y + x) = 3 (12x + y)$
$\Rightarrow 26x = 117y \Rightarrow x = \frac{117}{26}y = \frac{9}{2}y$.
Since $x < 12, y < 12$, so $x = 9, y = 2$.
Hence the man ordered $9$ feet $2$ inches of rope.

questions.question_text: A gardener has supply of fertilizer A which consists of 10% nitrogen and 6% phosphoric acid and fertilizer B which consists of 5% nitrogen and 10% phosphoric acid. After testing the soil conditions, he finds that he needs at least 14 kg of nitrogen and 14 kg of phosphoric acid for his crop. If fertilizer A costs ₹ 10.60 per kg and fertilizer B costs ₹ 8.40 per kg, what is the minimum cost at which the farmer can meet the nutrient requirement by using a combination of both types of fertilizers?
questions.solution:
Let the quantity of fertilizer A required be $x$ kg and that of fertilizer B be $y$ kg. Then,
$10\%$ of $x + 5\%$ of $y = 14 \Rightarrow 0.1 x + 0.05 y = 14$ ...(i)
$6\%$ of $x + 10\%$ of $y = 14 \Rightarrow 0.06 x + 0.1 y = 14$ ...(ii)
Subtracting (ii) from (i), we get: $0.04 x - 0.05 y = 0$
$\Rightarrow x = \frac{5}{4}y$.
Substituting $x = \frac{5}{4}y$ in (i), we get: $0.5y + 0.2y = 56$
$\Rightarrow 0.7 y = 56 \Rightarrow y = 80$.
Putting $y = 80$ in (ii), we get: $x = 100$.
$\therefore$ Required cost = ₹ $(100 \times 10.60 + 80 \times 8.40) =$ ₹ $1732$.

questions.question_text: I bought 5 pens, 7 pencils and 4 erasers. Rajan bought 6 pens, 8 erasers and 14 pencils for an amount which was half more than that I had paid. What percent of the total amount paid by me was paid for the pen?
questions.solution:
Let the individual cost of $1$ pen, $1$ pencil and $1$ eraser be ₹ $x$, ₹ $y$ and ₹ $z$ respectively.
Then, $5x + 7y + 4z = p$ (say) ...(i)
And, $6x + 14y + 8z = 1.5 p$ ...(ii)
Multiplying (i) by $2$ and subtracting (ii) from it, we get:
$4x = 0.5 p$ or $x = 0.125 p$.
$\therefore$ Required percentage = $\left(\frac{0.625 p}{p} \times 100\right) \% = 62.5\%$.

questions.question_text: The majority against a certain motion is equal to 8% of the total number of voting. If 14 of those who voted against it had voted for it, the motion would have been carried by 4 votes. Find the number of votes for and against the motion.
questions.solution:
Let the number of votes for the motion be $F$ and those against the motion be $A$.
Let the total number of votes be $x$.
Then, $(F + 14) - (A - 14) = 4 \Rightarrow A - F = 24$.
$\therefore 8\%$ of $x = 24 \Rightarrow x = \frac{24 \times 100}{8} = 300$.
So, $F + A = 300 \Rightarrow F + (F + 24) = 300$
$\Rightarrow 2F = 276 \Rightarrow F = 138$.
$\therefore A = (300 - 138) = 162$.

questions.question_text: The number of votes not cast for the Praja Party increased by 25% in the National General Election over those not cast for it in the previous Assembly polls and the Praja Party lost by a majority twice as large as that by which it had won the previous Assembly polls. If a total 2,60,000 people voted each time how many voted for the Praja Party in the previous Assembly polls?
questions.solution:
Let $x$ be the number of votes not cast for the Praja Party in the previous polls.
So, number of votes not cast for the party this year $= 1.25 x$.
Then, number of votes cast for Praja party in the previous polls $= (260000 - x)$.
Number of votes cast for the party this year $= (260000 - 1.25x)$.
Margin of victory in previous polls $= (\text{Votes cast in favour}) - (\text{Votes not cast in favour})$
$= (260000 - x) - x = (260000 - 2x)$.
Margin of loss in this year's polls $= (\text{Votes not cast in favour}) - (\text{Votes cast in favour})$
$= 1.25x - (260000 - 1.25x) = 2.5x - 260000$.
$\therefore 2.5x - 260000 = 2 (260000 - 2x) \Rightarrow 6.5x = 780000 \Rightarrow x = 120000$.
Hence, number of people who voted for Praja Party in previous polls $= 260000 - 120000 = 140000$.

questions.question_text: Solve $\left[180\%\text{ of } ?\right] \div 2 = 504$
questions.solution:
Given $(180 \%$ of $?) \div 2 = 504$
$\Rightarrow \left(? \times \frac{180}{100}\right) \div 2 = 504$
$\Rightarrow ? \times \frac{180}{100} = 504 \times 2$
$\Rightarrow ? \times \frac{180}{100} = 1008$
$= ? = \frac{1008 \times 100}{180} = 560$

questions.question_text:
What will come in the place of (?) in the expression below:
$x\%\text{ of } y \text{ is } y\%\text{ of } (?)$
questions.solution:
$x \%$ of $y = \frac{xy}{100}$ ...(i)
and $y \%$ of $x = \frac{xy}{100}$ ...(ii)
from (i) & (ii)
$x \%$ of $y = y \%$ of $x$

questions.question_text: How much $66\frac{2}{3}\%$ of ₹ 312 exceeds ₹ 200?
questions.solution:
$66 \frac{2}{3} \%$ of ₹ $312/-$ exceeds ₹ $200$ by ₹ $x$
According to the question, required difference
$= ₹ \left(312 \times \frac{200}{3 \times 100} - 200\right) = ₹ \left(312 \times \frac{200}{300} - 200\right)$
$= ₹ \left(200 \times \frac{312}{300} - 200\right)$
$\Rightarrow ₹ x = ₹ (208 - 200) = ₹ 8/-$

questions.question_text: State Electricity Board gives 15% discount on electric bills if it is paid before due date. One person gets ₹ 54 as discount. The amount of actual bill was:
questions.solution:
Let the amount of actual bill be ₹ $x$.
According to the question, $15\%$ of $x =$ ₹ $54$
$= \frac{x \times 15}{100} = 54$
$x = \frac{54 \times 100}{15} =$ ₹ $360$

Given $(550 \%$ of $250) \div 275 = (?)$
$(?) = \left(\frac{550 \times 250}{100}\right) \div 275$
$\Rightarrow (?) = (55 \times 25) \div 275$
$\Rightarrow (?) = \frac{55 \times 25}{275} = 5$

questions.passage_text:
Direction: In the question below consists of question statement and two statements I and II are given below it. You have to decide whether the data provided in the statement are sufficient to answer the question. Give answer.
(a) The data in statement I alone are sufficient to answer the question while II alone are not sufficient to answer the question.
(b) Data in statement II alone are sufficient to answer the question while data in statement I alone are not sufficient to answer the question.
(c) The data in statement I alone or in statement II alone are sufficient to answer the question.
(d) The data in both Statements I and II are not sufficient to answer the question.
(e) The data in both Statements I and II are sufficient to answer the question.
questions.question_text:
What is the minimum passing percentage in a test?
I. Raman scored 25% marks in the test and Sunil scored 288 marks which is 128 more than Raman.
II. Raman scored 64 marks less than the minimum passing marks.
questions.solution:
Statement I
Let maximum marks for test be $x$
Raman scored marks $= 288 - 128 = 160$ marks
According to question $25\%$ of $x = 160$
$\Rightarrow x = \frac{160 \times 100}{25} = 640$
Statement II : let minimum passing percentage be $y$ marks
Marks score by Raman $= 460$
Minimum passing marks $= 160 + 64 = 224$
According to question $y\%$ of $640 = 224$
$y = \frac{224 \times 100}{640} = 35\%$
Both Statement I and II together are necessary to answer the question
Hence option (e) is correct

questions.question_text: In a village 60% votes were cast in an election. A and B were the contestants. A won by 600 votes. If B had got 40% more votes, there would have been a tie between them. Find the number of recognized voters in the village.
questions.solution:
Let the number of recognized voters in the village be $x$.
A won election by votes $= 600$
B got $40\%$ more votes
For candidate B
$\Theta 40\% = 300$
$\therefore 100\% = \frac{300 \times 100}{40} = 750$
Votes got by B $= 750$
$\therefore$ Votes got by A $= 750 + 600 = 1350$
According to the question,
Number of votes cost in election $60\%$ of
$x = 1350 + 750 = 2100$
$\Rightarrow \frac{60}{100} x = 2100$
$\Rightarrow x = \frac{2100 \times 100}{60} = 3500$

questions.question_text: $\frac{4}{3}\text{ of } 25\%\text{ of } \frac{18}{19}\text{ of } 57 = ?$
questions.solution:
Given
$\frac{4}{3}$ of $25\%$ of $\frac{18}{19}$ of $57 = ?$
$? = \left[\left(\frac{25}{100} \times \frac{4}{3}\right) \times \left(\frac{18}{19} \times 57\right)\right]$
$= \left(\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{4}{3}\right) \times \left(\frac{18}{19} \times 57\right)$
$= 18$

questions.question_text: In an examination, 96% of students passed and 500 students failed. How many students did appear at the examination?
questions.solution:
Let number of students who appeared at the examination be $x$
Passed percentage $= 96\%$
Failed percentage $= 4\%$
According to given information we get
Unsuccessful students $\Rightarrow 4\%$
$\therefore 4\%$ of $x = 500$
$\Rightarrow \left(\frac{x \times 4}{100}\right) = 500$
$\Rightarrow x = \left(\frac{500 \times 100}{4}\right) = 12500$

questions.question_text: Madhur’s present salary is ₹ 3500. It will increase by 10% next year. What will be Madhur’s salary after the increment?
questions.solution:
$\Theta$ Krishna's present Salary = ₹ $3500$
Salary Increase by $10\%$
Increased salary of Krishna

Given

questions.question_text: Nagaraj could save 10% of his income. But 2 years later, when his income increased by 20%, he could save the same amount only as before. By how much percentage has his expenditure increased?
questions.solution:
Let, his income be ₹ $100$

Saving = ₹ $10$

Expenditure = ₹ $90$

Increased income = ₹ $120$

Increased expenditure = ₹ $110$

Increased in expenditure

$= \frac{110 - 90}{90}\times100$

$= \frac{200}{9} = 22\frac{2}{9}\%$

questions.question_text: The marked price of an article is ₹ 2400. The shopkeeper gives successive discounts of $x\%$ and 15% to the customer. If the customer pays ₹ 1876.8 for the article, find the value of $x$.
questions.solution:
Marked price of an article = ₹ $2400$

According to given information

$2400 \times (100 - x)\%$ of $85\% = 1876.80$

$\Rightarrow 2400 \times \frac{100 - x}{100} \times \frac{85}{100} = 1876.80$

$\Rightarrow (100 - x) = \frac{1876.80 \times 100 \times 100}{2400 \times 85}$

$= \frac{18768000}{204000} = 92$

$\Rightarrow (100 - x) = 92$

$\Rightarrow x = 100 - 92 = 8\%$

questions.question_text: Out of a total of 85 children playing Badminton or Table Tennis or both, total number of girls in the group is 70% of the total number of boys in the group, the number of boys playing only Badminton is 50% of the number of boys and total number of boys playing Badminton is 60% of the total number of boys. Number of children only playing table Tennis is 40% of the total number of children and a total of 12 children play both Badminton and Table Tennis. What is the number of girls playing only Badminton?
questions.solution:
Boys: Girls - $10 : 7$

$\therefore$ Boys $= \frac{10}{17}\times85 = 50$

Girls $= (85 - 50) = 35$

Boys who play badminton $50\%$ of $50 = \frac{50\times50}{100} = 25$

Boys who play badminton

$= \frac{60\times50}{100} = 30$

Children who play only table

Tennis $= \frac{85\times40}{100} = 34$

Children who play badminton and table tennis $= 12$

Boys who play only table tennis $= 50 - 30 = 20$

$\therefore$ Girls who play only table tennis $= 34 - 20 = 14$

Girls who play badminton and table tennis

$= 12 - (30 - 25) = 12 - 30 + 25 = 37 - 30 = 7$

$\therefore$ Girls who play only badminton

$= 35 - 14 - 7 = 35 - 21 = 14$

questions.question_text: Nandini Basu bought an article for ₹ 5844. She gave ₹ 156 to a mechanic to remove its defect. She then sold it for ₹ 5700. What was her loss per cent?
questions.solution:
Actual Cost price of an article

$= ₹ (5844 + 156)$

$= ₹ 6000$

S.P. $= ₹ 5700$

Loss $= ₹ (6000 - 5700) = ₹ 300$

Loss per cent

$= \frac{300}{6000}\times100 = 5\%$
